summary: One aggressive dogfood round for Bozz: discover, board meeting, A/B experiments, synthesize a plan

board.verdict: We have built a powerful engine and bolted it to the wrong chassis. The north star is "your morning in 90 seconds," yet a brand-new user lands on DEFAULT_HOME = [] (registry.tsx:111) and is handed FOUR sequential spotlight walkthroughs (Onboarding.tsx) that teach the tool (topics, widgets, sidebar reorder) instead of delivering the outcome. That is not a 90-second morning, it is a 15-step homework assignment. Meanwhile the single best asset we own is half-disconnected: the offline NLP parser (taskParser.ts: splitIntoTasks, extractDeadline, predictTopic) is wired ONLY to voice, so typing "essay due friday for bio" into QuickAddWidget just dumps the literal string with no date (QuickAddWidget.tsx:19, addToInbox(v, deadline)). And the one component that already computes the entire morning brief, the narrated line, overdue/today/week priorities, timed events, plan, TodayWidget.tsx, is just one optional card the user has to discover and place on an empty react-grid canvas. So we ship the morning brief as a kit, not as a briefing. The gap between promise and product is structural, not cosmetic, and that is exactly what dogfood v2 should attack: stop polishing the builder, ship the briefing. I am at a 5: the foundations are genuinely strong (parser, TodayWidget, worlds/templates, cold-start chips all exist), which is why this round is about wiring what we already have into the promise, not net-new infrastructure. We are one bold round away from the product finally being what the tagline says.
topPriorities: BRIEFING AS THE DEFAULT LANDING SURFACE (boldness 5). Make the zero-config 'Today' briefing the literal first screen on launch, not a widget you assemble. TodayWidget.tsx already computes everything: narrated line up top ('1 overdue, 2 due today, next class 14:00'), then schedule, then what's due, then ONE primary action. The customizable grid demotes to an optional 'Board' for power users. This is THE north-star move: an impatient student gets the 90-second glance on open without ever touching edit mode. Reframes us from 'a dashboard you configure' to 'a briefing that runs you' (no rival owns this).

TYPED QUICK-ADD = THE VOICE PARSER (boldness 4). Run extractDeadline + predictTopic on the typed QuickAddWidget string so 'essay due friday for bio' instantly becomes {text:'essay', deadline:Fri, topic:Bio} with an inline confirm chip, and auto-route on confirm when confidence is high, skipping the Quicks middle-step. TodayWidget's dueRows already auto-surfaces dated tasks, so a captured deadline appears in the morning brief with ZERO planner ceremony. This collapses capture to triage to plan into one keystroke and makes the planner optional instead of mandatory. Lowest effort, highest leverage: the parser already exists, this is wiring.

FIRST-CLASS TIMETABLE BUILDER (boldness 4). Onboarding sells 'your real classes fill Today,' but the only way in is pasting a (often broken) read-only ICS, and CalendarNote supports only single one-off events. Add a recurring CalendarNote variant (weekday + start/end + room) expanded client-side in calendar.ts the same way topicDeadlineEvents already synthesizes events, plus a location field surfaced in Today, and a persistent 'Next: BIO101 14:00 Room 3.21 in 18 min' banner off the nextEvent data TodayWidget already has. This is the difference between a glorified ICS viewer and the one thing a student checks before leaving the house.

KILL THE FOUR-WALKTHROUGH GATE, SHIP A 60-SECOND OUTCOME SETUP (boldness 4). Replace the four abstract spotlight tours (Onboarding.tsx) with one outcome-driven flow: (1) paste/build timetable, (2) add your first 3 deadlines via the new NL quick-add, (3) done, here is your morning. Wire the existing cold-start chips + STARTER_TEMPLATES/worldToTopic so she RECEIVES a populated briefing rather than constructing it. Demote the tours to re-runnable 'learn more' in Settings. Dependent on priorities 1 and 2, do it last in the round so it lands the user directly on the real briefing.

board.role: CPO (Product) — activation, retention, the core daily loop
board.verdict: Bozz has a genuinely differentiated engine and is quietly under-shipping it. The two best assets in this category — the offline NL parser (splitIntoTasks/extractDeadline/predictTopic in taskParser.ts) and the auto-computed morning brief (TodayWidget.tsx) — already exist and are good. The problem is they are gated, voice-only, or buried, so the north star ("your morning in 90 seconds") is technically present but not FELT. Three concrete dead-ends prove this. (1) The parser is wired ONLY to voice: QuickAddWidget.submit() calls addToInbox(text, deadline) with the literal string, so typing "essay due friday" stores raw text with no date — the magic is invisible at the exact moment a typing-first student would feel it. (2) The morning brief is one optional card on DEFAULT_HOME = [] (registry.tsx:111); a cold-start user gets a seeded grid via chooseColdStart, but a SKIP user (skipColdStart) still lands on an empty canvas behind four tool-teaching walkthroughs ("A topic is an area of your life"). (3) The onboarding sells "your real classes fill Today" but CalendarNote (types.ts) has no recurrence and no location field — the timetable killer feature dead-ends at one-off ICS events. We are a dogfood loop into v2 (AGGRESSIVE) and the prior rounds did smart plumbing (cold-start seeding, dueRows dedup, empty-state templates) but stopped short of the bold reframes. The activation funnel still has too many screens between capture and "it showed up in my morning," and the product still asks the user to ASSEMBLE the promise instead of RECEIVING it. This round we close those gaps decisively. I'm at a 5: strong bones, weak felt-payoff, and the highest-leverage fixes are wiring + composition over existing state, not new infra — which means there's no excuse not to ship them.
topPriorities: P1 — Wire the NL parser into TYPED quick-add (the single highest-leverage fix, ~1 file). In QuickAddWidget.submit(), run the existing extractDeadline + predictTopic on the typed string so 'essay due friday for bio' instantly resolves to {text:'essay', deadline:Fri, topic:Bio} shown as an inline confirm chip; on confirm auto-route to the predicted topic (skip the Quicks middle-step when confidence is high). TodayWidget's dueRows already surfaces dated tasks in the morning brief with zero planner ceremony, so this alone collapses capture→triage→plan into one keystroke and makes the planner optional. Pure wiring over taskParser.ts + addToInbox/onAssign — no schema, no infra.

P2 — Make a zero-config 'Today' briefing the DEFAULT landing surface, not a card to find. TodayWidget already computes the entire brief; ship it full-width as the literal default home (narrated line up top, schedule, what's due, one primary action) and demote the customizable react-grid canvas to an optional 'Board'. Critically, fix the SKIP path: skipColdStart() currently lands on an empty grid + four walkthroughs — route every new account (cold-start AND skip) onto the briefing so an impatient student gets the 90-second glance on launch without ever entering edit mode. Composition over existing WidgetCtx; gate behind a default-section flag so existing users' saved layouts are untouched (hard constraint: don't disrupt returning users).

P3 — Make the timetable first-class so the onboarding promise stops dead-ending. Add a recurring CalendarNote variant (weekday + start/end + room/location) expanded client-side in lib/calendar.ts the same way topicDeadlineEvents already synthesizes events, add a `location` field to CalendarEvent surfaced in TodayWidget, and a persistent 'Next: BIO101 · 14:00 · Room 3.21 · in 18 min' banner driven off the nextEvent already computed in TodayWidget. This is the difference between a glorified ICS viewer and the one place a student checks before leaving the house. Self-contained types + client-side expansion; touches no auth/schema/sync.

P4 — Replace the four tool-teaching walkthroughs with a 60-second outcome-driven setup, and surface the parser's wow on a zero-topic account. Collapse onboarding to: (1) paste timetable [exists], (2) add your first 3 deadlines via the P1 NL quick-add, (3) here's your morning. Kill the spotlight tours from the default path (keep re-runnable from Settings). AND fix the cold-first-capture: predictTopic returns null at topics.length===0, so infer a domain via the existing DOMAIN_KEYWORDS map and offer a one-tap 'Create Job hunt topic and file this here' chip, converting the dead first capture into the aha moment. Onboarding.tsx + InboxView.tsx + taskParser.ts over existing state.

board.verdict: The codebase is in genuinely good engineering shape: TypeScript compiles green, lib/ logic is cleanly separated from presentation, the offline NLP engine (taskParser) is a real asset, and the widget context seam keeps additions self-contained. Crucially, three prior dogfood-v2 rounds have ALREADY landed at HEAD (commits 5a1a8fc, 0ab2afc, 72e4ad6): the morning brief is real (TodayWidget computes narrated summary + overdue/today/week priorities + dueRows), deadlines are trustworthy (DatePicker/DeadlineControl rework), the timetable front door exists (WelcomeTimetable wrapping AddFeedForm), cold-start seeds real topics, and the capture-to-surface seams are wired (DeadlineQuickAdd, HomeCoachChip, QuickAddModal already runs parseVoiceTasks with live preview). So a large fraction of the board's "empty canvas / four walkthroughs / no brief" complaints describe a state we have already moved past — the deck was generated against an older snapshot. My satisfaction is a 6, not higher, because the product still dead-ends on its single most-sold feature: the timetable is read-only ICS over a one-off CalendarNote model with no recurring class, no room/location, and no live next-class countdown — the onboarding sells "your real classes fill Today" and the data model can't actually hold a weekly class. The other real, unshipped gaps are model-shaped: QuickAddWidget (the home grid widget, distinct from the Ctrl+B modal) still calls addToInbox with raw text and a manual datepicker, so it bypasses the parser the rest of the app already uses; there is no grade/module concept anywhere; TopicItem has no recurrence; and Ctrl+K is still only a section-jumper. My engineering concern with the boldest proposals (time logs, billable rates, grades, budget-topic links, recurring classes all at once) is data-model sprawl — five "just add a field to types.ts" changes landed carelessly will fragment the model and rot the sync blob. I will back bold, but each bold change must extend the model coherently (expanded client-side like topicDeadlineEvents already does, not a parallel store) and keep TS green. This round I want depth on the killer student gap and on closing the capture parity hole, not a scatter of half-built personas.
topPriorities: Make the timetable first-class via a recurring-class model. Add a recurring CalendarNote variant (weekday + start/end + room/location) expanded client-side in lib/calendar.ts the same way topicDeadlineEvents() already synthesizes events — NOT a parallel data store. Surface location in TodayWidget and add the persistent 'Next: BIO101 14:00 Room 3.21 in 18 min' banner driven off the nextEvent already computed in TodayWidget. This is the one feature the onboarding sells that the data model literally cannot hold today; fixing it converts Bozz from a glorified ICS viewer into the thing a student checks before leaving the house. Boldest justified bet of the round.

Close the capture-parity hole: wire extractDeadline + predictTopic into QuickAddWidget so typing 'essay due friday for bio' in the home widget parses date + topic with an inline confirm chip, exactly like QuickAddModal already does. This is pure reuse of shipped, tested logic (zero new infra, zero schema change), removes a glaring inconsistency where the same typed string behaves differently in two surfaces, and is the lowest-risk high-value change available — it should land early and green.

Turn Ctrl+K into a real command palette as the keyboard spine: index every topic + topic task (not just budget/inbox/calendar as today), and add an action layer ('add <text> #topic ^friday', 'done <task>', 'go <topic>') routed through the EXISTING parser and the existing mutators (addTopicItem, onAdvanceStage). This is wiring over infrastructure that already exists (Fuse, taskParser, Dashboard mutators), it compounds the value of every other feature, and it is self-contained React/TS that keeps the model untouched.

Ship a student-native Modules & grades layer as ONE coherent model extension, not a grab-bag. Let a topic flag itself a 'course' and let its items carry weight% + grade, then a Modules widget with a running weighted grade / 'what do I need on the final' calc, reusing the existing deadlineEntries data for a 2-week crunch heatmap. I rank this fourth and explicitly scope it tight: it is the single most student-specific missing feature and a strong reason a student keeps a separate spreadsheet, but it is also where data-model sprawl risk is highest, so it must extend Topic/TopicItem with optional fields (backward-compatible, no migration, no sync-blob bloat) and ship behind the course flag so non-course topics are untouched.

board.verdict:
Bozz today has world-class plumbing and no wow moment. From a growth seat that is the worst possible combination: the asset that should sell this app by word of mouth, the offline NL parser (extractDeadline + predictTopic + splitIntoTasks in src/lib/taskParser.ts), is genuinely better than what Notion/Sunsama/Akiflow charge a subscription for, and it is INVISIBLE on the surfaces a new student actually touches. I verified the smoking gun: QuickAddWidget.submit() (src/components/widgets/QuickAddWidget.tsx:16-24) calls addToInbox(v, deadline) with the raw typed string, so "essay due friday for bio" is stored as literal text with no date and no topic. The parser is wired only to voice. So the most-used capture path throws away the one thing that would make a student go "wait, how did it know that."

The first-run is worse. DEFAULT_HOME is literally [] (registry.tsx:111), so a brand-new account lands on an empty canvas gated behind FOUR sequential walkthroughs (Onboarding.tsx) whose own copy is product-internal ("A topic is an area of your life," "use the eye to hide a topic and the dots to drag it"). That is 15-20 manual build steps teaching the TOOL before the user gets any OUTCOME. The north star is "your morning in 90 seconds" and we ship "here is a builder, assemble your morning yourself." The TodayWidget already computes the entire 90-second brief (narrated line, overdue/today/week, next event) but it is one optional card a user has to discover and place. We have the magic and the promise; we are hiding both behind a furniture-assembly tutorial. No competitor owns the morning ritual, and we are choosing not to either. There is also zero shareability layer (ReviewView exists with weeklyStats but nothing exportable, no streaks), so even the users who get value have nothing to post in the group chat. For a student app, organic acquisition IS the strategy and right now there is no viral surface at all. The bones are a 9; the felt experience for a cold student is a 4.
topPriorities: WIRE THE PARSER INTO THE WOW MOMENT (highest leverage, smallest change). Run the existing extractDeadline + predictTopic on the TYPED quick-add string in QuickAddWidget.tsx and QuickAddModal, not just voice. Show a live inline confirm chip ('essay  due Fri  Bio') as they type, and on a zero-topic account surface a one-tap 'Create Bio topic and file this here' chip (DOMAIN_KEYWORDS already exists, predictTopic just returns null when topics.length===0). This converts the dead first-capture into the aha moment using code we already shipped. This is the single thing most likely to generate word of mouth.

MAKE THE 90-SECOND MORNING THE LITERAL DEFAULT LANDING SURFACE. Replace the empty DEFAULT_HOME canvas with a zero-config full-width 'Today' briefing built on the data TodayWidget already computes: big narrated line up top ('1 overdue · 2 due today · next class 14:00'), then schedule, then what's due, then ONE primary action. Keep the customizable grid as the optional 'Board' for power users. The promise has to be felt on launch, not assembled.

KILL THE FOUR-WALKTHROUGH GATE; ship a 60-second outcome-driven setup in student language. Two or three questions ('What's on your plate? Uni / Job hunt / Money / Fitness', 'When's your next deadline?') that auto-generate a populated home + 1-2 seeded topics in one tap (STARTER_TEMPLATES + worldToTopic already exist). She should RECEIVE her morning view, not construct it. Demote the spotlight tours to optional 'learn more' from Settings.

STAND UP A SHAREABILITY / RETENTION LAYER so value escapes the app. A Spotify-Wrapped-style weekly card exported as an image (ReviewView already computes weeklyStats: tasks done, deadlines beaten, top topic) plus a momentum streak on the morning brief (Habit.entries + completedAt make the math trivial). Students are the BeReal/Duolingo generation; a postable 'This week: 23 tasks done, longest streak 6 days' card is free acquisition inside a class group chat, a lane Notion/Things deliberately won't touch.

board.verdict: Bozz today is a beautifully built widget construction kit that is impersonating a morning briefing. The craft is real: TodayWidget.tsx already computes the entire 90-second brief (narrated summary line, overdue/today/week priorities, timed events, plan), the offline taskParser.ts (splitIntoTasks + predictTopic + extractDeadline) is a genuine moat that competitors charge monthly for, and the visual system (glass, accents, spotlight onboarding) is polished. But the product fights its own north star at every first-touch surface. DEFAULT_HOME is literally [] (registry.tsx:111), so a new student lands on an empty canvas behind a "Getting started" card that hands them FOUR sequential walkthroughs they are told to "do them in order" (Onboarding.tsx:324) — and those walkthroughs teach product-internal vocabulary ("A topic is an area of your life," "use the eye to hide a topic and the dots to drag it") instead of the outcome. The promise is "your morning in 90 seconds"; the experience is "build your own morning in 20 clicks." Worse, the single best asset is unwired at the exact moment it should wow: QuickAddWidget.tsx calls addToInbox(v, deadline) with the raw literal string — typing "essay due friday for bio" stores "essay due friday for bio" with no date and no topic, even though taskParser already turns it into {text:'essay', deadline:Fri, topic:Bio} for voice. And the timetable the whole WelcomeTimetable onboarding SELLS dead-ends: CalendarNote (types.ts:282) has no recurrence and no location field, so a student can paste a read-only ICS but cannot build "BIO101, Tue 14:00, Room 3.21, every week." The feel is high-craft on the inside, high-friction at the threshold. We are one round of decisive composition (not new infra) away from the product actually delivering its tagline on launch — almost everything needed already exists in WidgetCtx and the parser.
topPriorities: Ship the briefing as the DEFAULT landing surface, not a widget you assemble. Set DEFAULT_HOME to a populated layout (TodayWidget full-width + quickAdd) so a brand-new account opens to the morning brief that TodayWidget.tsx already computes — narrated line on top, schedule, what's due, one primary action — instead of an empty react-grid canvas. Keep the customizable grid as an optional 'Board'. This is the difference between receiving your morning and building it, and it's pure composition over existing state.

Wire the parser into typed capture so capture==triage==plan is one keystroke. Run the existing extractDeadline + predictTopic on the QuickAddWidget string (and QuickCapture/Ctrl+B), show a live token-highlighted confirm chip ('essay · Bio · Fri') as the user types, and auto-route on Enter when confidence is high — skipping the Quicks middle-step. A dated task then auto-surfaces in TodayWidget's dueRows with zero planner ceremony. The NLP is shipped and excellent; today the UI throws it away for typed input.

Make the timetable first-class to honour what onboarding promises. Add a recurring CalendarNote variant (weekday + start/end, expanded client-side in lib/calendar.ts the same way topicDeadlineEvents already synthesizes events) plus a `location`/room field surfaced in TodayWidget. Add a persistent 'Next: BIO101 · 14:00 · Room 3.21 · in 18 min' countdown banner driven off the nextEvent data TodayWidget already derives. This turns a glorified ICS viewer into the one thing a student checks before leaving the house.

Replace the four tool-teaching walkthroughs with one 60-second outcome flow in student language. Collapse onboarding to: (1) paste timetable [exists], (2) add your first 3 deadlines via the NL quick-add, (3) here's your morning. Kill the spotlight tours from the default path; keep them re-runnable from Settings for power users. 'Do them in order' before the app does anything is exactly what makes an impatient student bounce — the onboarding should hand them a populated morning, not four homework assignments.

board.verdict: Bozz today is a strong toolkit wearing the wrong shape. The engine room is genuinely good and under-shipped: TodayWidget.tsx already computes the entire "morning in 90 seconds" (narrated summary line, overdue/today/week buckets, timed events, dueRows that auto-surface dated tasks), and taskParser.ts has a real offline NL pipeline (splitIntoTasks/predictTopic/extractDeadline) with zero API cost. Both are assets a competitor would pay for. But the product wraps them in a builder, not a briefing: DEFAULT_HOME is literally [] (registry.tsx:111), so a new student lands on an empty react-grid canvas behind four "do them in order" walkthroughs, and the morning brief is just one optional card she has to find and place. Worse, the NL parser is wired ONLY to voice — typing "essay due friday" in QuickAddWidget.tsx (line 19, addToInbox(v, deadline)) stores the literal string with no date and no topic. So the two best things we own are invisible at exactly the moments that decide retention (first launch, first capture). My read as COO: we do not have a value problem, we have a packaging-and-focus problem. The data model is also too generic for our locked primary user — CalendarNote (types.ts:282) is single-day-only with no recurrence and no location, and Topic/TopicItem have no grade/weight, so the timetable feature the onboarding SELLS dead-ends and grades (the #1 reason a student keeps a separate spreadsheet) aren't modelled at all. This is dogfood v2 (AGGRESSIVE) and the mandate is bold, so we should stop polishing the canvas and reframe the product around the promise. Cut scope hard: the freelancer/multi-client cluster (Client Pulse, billable time, client switcher, per-client budget rollups, Freelancer World) is a coherent and tempting second product, but it serves Tom, not Maya/Priya, our locked primary user. I am explicitly DEFERRING that entire cluster this round — chasing two personas in one aggressive sprint is how we ship breadth and lose the north star.
topPriorities: SHIP THE BRIEFING AS THE DEFAULT SURFACE (boldness 5). Make the auto-computed morning brief the literal landing page, not a card you assemble. TodayWidget.tsx already produces the narrated line + priorities + events + plan; promote it to a full-width, zero-config 'Today' that opens on launch, with the customizable grid demoted to an optional 'Board'. This single move converts our best-built asset from hidden to default and is the most direct delivery of 'your morning in 90 seconds.' No new data needed — pure composition over existing state.

WIRE THE NL PARSER INTO TYPED QUICK-ADD + COLD-START CAPTURE (boldness 4). Run extractDeadline + predictTopic on the typed QuickAddWidget string so 'essay due friday for bio' instantly parses to {text, deadline, topic} with an inline confirm chip, auto-routing on high confidence and skipping the Quicks middle-step. Pair it with the zero-topic fix: on a fresh account, infer a domain via DOMAIN_KEYWORDS (predictTopic returns null at 0 topics today) and offer a one-tap 'Create Job hunt and file this here' chip. This makes our hidden superpower fire on the first keystroke and first capture — the aha moments we currently waste.

MAKE THE STUDENT DATA MODEL FIRST-CLASS: recurring timetable + grades (boldness 5). Add a recurring CalendarNote variant (weekday + start/end + room/location) expanded client-side in lib/calendar.ts the same way deadlines are synthesized, and surface location + a 'Next: BIO101 · 14:00 · Room 3.21 · in 18 min' banner in the briefing. Let a topic flag as a 'course' so items carry weight% + grade, and ship a lightweight Modules view with a running weighted grade. This closes the dead-end the onboarding already sells and gives a student the one thing they currently can't get from their calendar app — the reason to drop the spreadsheet.

REPLACE ONBOARDING WITH A 60-SECOND OUTCOME FLOW (boldness 4). Kill the four spotlight tours from the default path (keep them re-runnable in Settings). Collapse first-run to: paste timetable [exists] → add your first 3 deadlines via the new NL quick-add → 'here's your morning' lands her ON the briefing already populated. Wire the existing cold-start chips so she RECEIVES her morning view instead of constructing it. The current 'do them in order' homework gate is exactly what bounces an impatient first-year before the app does anything.

opportunities.title: Make the timetable first-class: manual + recurring class entry, room/location, and a live "next class in 20 min" countdown
opportunities.rationale: The entire onboarding (WelcomeTimetable.tsx) sells "your real classes fill Today," but the only way to get a timetable in is pasting an ICS link via AddFeedForm.tsx — a read-only subscription. As Maya, my uni either gives a broken/clunky ICS or none, and CalendarNote (lib/types.ts) only supports single, one-off events: there is no recurring event, no location/room field, no class-vs-task distinction. So the killer feature dead-ends. BOLD FIX: add a proper timetable builder — create a class once with a weekday + start/end + room and have it repeat every week (a new recurring CalendarNote variant, expanded client-side in lib/calendar.ts the same way topicDeadlineEvents already synthesizes events). Add a `location` field surfaced in TodayScheduleWidget/TodayWidget. Then add a persistent "Next: BIO101 · 14:00 · Room 3.21 · in 18 min" banner driven off todayEvents (the data is already computed in TodayWidget.tsx via nextEvent). This is the difference between a glorified ICS viewer and the one place a student actually checks before leaving the house.

opportunities.title: Collapse capture→triage→plan into one move: natural-language quick-add that dates, routes, and surfaces a task in a single keystroke
opportunities.area: QuickAdd widget + Quicks inbox + Daily Planner
opportunities.rationale: Right now a task crosses three screens before it shows in my morning: QuickAddWidget.tsx dumps raw text to Quicks → InboxView.tsx makes me pick a topic + date → DailyPlannerView.tsx makes me drag it onto a day before it appears in "My plan." An impatient first-year will never finish that. Critically, the powerful NL parser in taskParser.ts (splitIntoTasks, extractDeadline, predictTopic) is wired ONLY to voice — typing "essay due friday" in QuickAdd stores the literal string with no date. BOLD FIX: run the existing extractDeadline + predictTopic on the typed quick-add string so "essay due friday for bio" instantly becomes {text:'essay', deadline:Fri, topic:Bio} with an inline confirm chip; auto-route it on confirm (skip the Quicks middle-step when confidence is high). TodayWidget already auto-surfaces due-today/overdue deadlines (the dueRows logic), so a dated task appears in the morning brief with ZERO planner ceremony. This makes the planner optional instead of mandatory and delivers the 90-second promise for the impatient user.

opportunities.title: Add a student-native "Modules & grades" structure on top of topics, with a deadline-stress view
opportunities.rationale: Topics (lib/types.ts) are deliberately generic ("an area of your life like Uni, Work, the gym") and the cold-start makes ONE topic called 'Uni'. But a real student lives in MODULES (BIO101, CS102), each with its own assignments, weightings, and grades — none of which the app models. There's no grade tracking anywhere, which is arguably the single most student-specific feature missing, and a strong reason a student keeps a separate spreadsheet. BOLD FIX: let a topic flag itself as a "course" so its items can carry weight % + grade, and ship a Modules widget that shows each module, its next deadline, and a running weighted grade / "what do I need on the final" calc. Pair it with a "crunch radar" — a heatmap of the next 2 weeks by deadline density (data already exists via deadlineEntries used in TodayWidget.tsx) so I can SEE that week 6 is hell before it arrives. This turns Bozz from a generic dashboard into the thing a student actually can't get from their calendar app.

opportunities.title: Replace the four abstract walkthroughs with a 60-second "set up my week" flow in student language
opportunities.rationale: After the (good) cold-start chips, Onboarding.tsx drops me into four spotlight tours whose vocabulary is product-internal: "A topic is an area of your life," "add the widgets you want on that topic's page," "use the eye to hide a topic and the dots to drag it." As Maya I do not care about topics, widgets, or sidebar reordering on day one — I care about "when's my next class" and "what's due this week." The walkthroughs teach the tool, not the outcome. BOLD FIX: collapse onboarding into one outcome-driven flow — (1) paste timetable [already exists], (2) add my first 3 deadlines via the NL quick-add (opportunity #2), (3) done — here's your morning. Kill the spotlight tours from the default path (keep them re-runnable from Settings for power users). The current Getting Started card even says "Do them in order" — four homework assignments before the app does anything is exactly what makes an impatient student bounce.

opportunities.title: A genuine "morning in 90 seconds" briefing mode — one full-screen, glanceable daily card instead of a widget grid to assemble
opportunities.rationale: The north star is "your morning in 90 seconds," but the home (HomeView.tsx) is an empty react-grid-layout canvas the user must assemble from an Add-widget panel, with edit mode, resize handles, corner/border controls, color dropdowns — a builder, not a briefing. The excellent TodayWidget.tsx already computes the entire morning brief (narrated summary line, overdue/today/week priorities, timed events, plan) — it's just one optional card among many I have to find and place. BOLD FIX: ship a dedicated, zero-config "Today" briefing as the literal default landing surface: full-width, big type, the narrated line up top ("1 overdue · 2 due today · next class 14:00"), then class schedule, then what's due, then a single primary action (advance / mark done / capture). The customizable grid stays as the optional "Board" for people who want it. Defaulting to the briefing means an impatient student gets the promised 90-second glance on launch without ever entering edit mode.

opportunities.title: Link assignments to their module's classes so a deadline knows where it came from
opportunities.rationale: A lecture event (from the ICS feed or a manual class) and the assignment for that same module are completely disconnected: events come from feedEvents/CalendarNotes, deadlines come from topic items (topicDeadlineEvents in lib/calendar.ts). As a student my mental model is "BIO101 has a lecture Tue 9am AND an essay due Friday" — one thing. BOLD FIX (smaller, high-leverage): when I add a deadline I can tag it to a module/topic colour (already happens), and in Today/Calendar show a class event with a subtle badge if that module has work due soon ("BIO101 · 14:00 · 1 due Fri"). It closes the loop between "where I have to be" and "what I owe," which is the exact glue a calendar app can't provide and is cheap to build on the existing colour/topic linkage.

opportunities.title: Client Pulse: a cross-client status board that replaces the single Summary widget
opportunities.rationale: As Tom I juggle 4 clients and the ONLY aggregate view today is SummaryWidget (src/components/widgets/SummaryWidget.tsx), which sums ALL topics into three numbers (doing/todo/done) and discards which client is which. TodayWidget interleaves deadlines from every client with only a 5px colour dot to tell them apart. To answer 'where does each client stand' I must click into each topic page one by one. Build a 'Client Pulse' widget: one row per topic (client), each showing its colour, next deadline (overdue in red), count by stage, and whether anything is overdue, sorted so the client on fire floats to top. Data already exists per Topic (stages, items, deadline/dueMin via deadlineEntries in widgets/util) and ctx.topics is already passed to widgets, so this is self-contained React/TS reading existing state. This is the at-a-glance status I actually open the app for, and it directly delivers the 'your morning in 90 seconds' promise for a multi-client user instead of a single-life user.

opportunities.title: Billable time tracking tied to topics (clients) — start/stop timer + logged hours
opportunities.area: New feature: per-topic time logs + revamped Pomodoro
opportunities.rationale: A grep across src for invoice|timetrack|billable|hourly|rate returns nothing — there is no concept of time or money-per-client anywhere. PomodoroWidget (src/components/widgets/PomodoroWidget.tsx) keeps a session count only in local state, isn't linked to any topic, and resets entirely on unmount. For a freelancer, tracked time IS the product — it becomes the invoice. Add an optional hourlyRate to Topic and a timeLogs array (topicId, start, durationMin) persisted like other Dashboard state, plus a 'Focus' widget that lets me pick a client, run a timer, and log the block against that client. Surface 'X hrs this week · £Y' per client in the Client Pulse row. Self-contained: new types in src/lib/types.ts, new state slice in Dashboard.tsx mirroring dailyPlan/habits, a new widget in the registry. Touches no auth/payments/OAuth/schema. This is the single highest-value thing missing for the freelancer persona.

opportunities.title: Client switcher / focus mode — jump and filter the whole app by active client
opportunities.rationale: Navigating 4 clients today means clicking each topic in the sidebar individually (Dashboard.tsx navItems), and there's no way to say 'today I'm only working on Client B' and have Home, Today, deadlines and the timer all narrow to that client. Add a lightweight client switcher (a pill row or Cmd+number shortcut — Dashboard already owns a global keydown handler for Cmd+K/Cmd+B at lines ~620-637) plus a 'focus on this client' toggle that filters TodayWidget priorities/tasks and the new Client Pulse to a single topic. For someone living in browser tabs who context-switches between clients all day, a one-keystroke client jump is the speed feature that makes Bozz feel native to freelance work. Fully implementable in React/TS over existing topic state.

opportunities.title: Smarter, client-aware capture: auto-seed routing keywords from client names + a per-client quick-add
opportunities.rationale: predictTopic (src/lib/taskParser.ts) scores captures against generic DOMAIN_KEYWORDS (music/work/fitness/etc) and the topic NAME, but a client named 'Acme' or a person's name won't match my notes about them unless I manually add keywords per topic. As Tom most of my quicks are 'Acme — send revised logo' or 'ping Sarah re invoice'. Two bold improvements: (1) when a topic is created, auto-seed keywords from the client name AND learn from history — every time I file a quick into a client, remember the distinctive words so future captures of the same phrasing auto-predict (a local learned-keyword map persisted alongside topics, still zero-API/offline). (2) Add a '@client' fast-path in capture so typing the client name routes instantly. This sharpens the existing InboxView 'Accept all predicted' flow (src/components/sections/InboxView.tsx) so triage of a day's brain-dump across 4 clients is near-zero-effort.

opportunities.title: A real Freelancer World/template: client folder structure + client-pipeline stages + portfolio home in one tap
opportunities.rationale: The 'Freelancer' starter (registry.tsx STARTER_TEMPLATES) is just Today+Budget+Clock+QuickAdd — generic, and the cold-start options (src/lib/coldStart.ts) only offer Uni/Job hunt/Gym/Work, none of which model 'I have N clients'. A freelancer's mental model is a roster of clients, each a pipeline (Brief → In progress → Review → Invoiced → Paid). Ship a Freelancer cold-start/World that spins up a 'Clients' folder, prompts me to name my clients, creates a colour-coded topic per client pre-loaded with freelance-appropriate stages (including an 'Invoiced'/'Paid' done-stage so the pipeline tracks money), and lands me on a portfolio home featuring the new Client Pulse + time/budget widgets. worldToTopic + custom stages are already supported (BozzWorld.stages in types.ts), so this composes existing machinery into a first-run that makes Bozz feel purpose-built for me in 90 seconds.

opportunities.title: Connect Budget to clients: per-client income, outstanding invoices, and 'owed to me' rollups
opportunities.rationale: BudgetData (src/lib/types.ts) already has TransactionType including 'owed-to-me' and 'i-owe', but transactions carry only a free-text category — there is no link to a topic, so I cannot answer 'how much has Acme paid me this year / what's outstanding?'. Add an optional topicId to OneOffTransaction/RecurringItem and surface a per-client money line (earned MTD, outstanding 'owed-to-me') in the Client Pulse widget and on each client's topic page. Combined with the billable-time feature, this closes the loop from tracked hours to expected income per client — the financial at-a-glance a freelancer needs most. Self-contained data + UI change; no payments, schema, or OAuth involvement.

opportunities.title: Ship a real "90-second morning" instead of an empty canvas + four homework walkthroughs
opportunities.rationale: The north star is "your morning in 90 seconds," but DEFAULT_HOME is literally [] (registry.tsx:111) and a brand-new account lands on HomeView's empty state behind a "Getting started" card offering FOUR sequential walkthroughs the user is told to "do in order" (Onboarding.tsx:124-153, 324). Each walkthrough makes Priya drive the UI herself (open Edit, click +, pick widgets, connect accounts). For a non-techy user who stalls without a next step, that is 15-20 manual build steps before she sees ANY value, the opposite of the promise. BOLD MOVE: replace the build-it-yourself onboarding with a 2-3 question conversational setup ("What's on your plate? Uni / Job hunt / Money / Fitness…", "When's your next deadline?") that auto-generates a populated Home AND 1-2 seeded topics with stages in one tap, then drops her on a home that already shows today, deadlines, and a filled quick-add. The plumbing exists: STARTER_TEMPLATES + applyTemplate (HomeView.tsx:189-200) and worldToTopic/worldToFolder (WorldsView.tsx). Wire the questions to those so she RECEIVES her morning view rather than constructing it. Keep the walkthroughs as optional "learn more," not the gate.

opportunities.title: Make Quicks' prediction magic visible on a zero-topic account (the hook fires too late)
opportunities.rationale: The capture-then-triage intelligence is the app's best idea and is genuinely good: parseVoiceTasks splits a ramble into tasks with predicted topic + deadline, and InboxView re-predicts live and offers "Accept all predicted" (taskParser.ts:405, InboxView.tsx:190-238). BUT predictTopic returns null when topics.length === 0 (taskParser.ts:263), and a fresh user HAS no topics, so the first capture, the exact moment that should wow her, shows a bare row with a disabled-feeling "new topic" button and a help banner pointing to Settings (InboxView.tsx:239-249). The wow is invisible precisely when it matters. BOLD MOVE: on a zero/low-topic account, have capture still infer a domain via the existing DOMAIN_KEYWORDS map (taskParser.ts:190-205, currently only used to score EXISTING topics) and surface a one-tap "Create 'Job hunt' topic and file this here" chip with a suggested icon/colour/stages, so her very first quick spins up a correctly-named topic automatically. This converts the dead first-capture into the aha moment.

opportunities.title: Offer a "simple list" topic mode so stages don't gatekeep non-techy users
opportunities.rationale: Creating a topic forces Priya through a Kanban mental model: the topic modal centres on "Stages" (the steps a task moves through, TopicFolderEditModal.tsx:209-241) plus description, icon and colour, and DailyPlanner/TopicTodos are built around stage advancement (DailyPlannerView.tsx:173-191). Stages are a project-management concept a 19-year-old organising her life does not have and was not asked if she wants. There is no "I just want a checklist" path; every topic is a pipeline. BOLD MOVE: add a topic-level "simple list" toggle (default ON for the primary student persona) that collapses stages to a single done/not-done checkbox everywhere it renders, and only reveals the multi-stage pipeline when she opts into "track stages." This is self-contained in React/TS (a flag on Topic + conditional rendering in TopicView/DailyPlanner/TopicTodos) and removes the single biggest conceptual barrier to her ever finishing setup.

opportunities.title: Auto-build the morning brief: one daily "here's your day" surface generated from her data
opportunities.rationale: Right now "your morning" is assembled from independent widgets she must add and arrange (today, upcomingDeadlines, dailyPlanner, recentEmails). The greeting line teases the framing ("good morning. here is your day.", HomeView.tsx:38-43) and HomeCoachChip already computes the right signals: no calendar feed, quicks waiting, nothing planned, no email (HomeCoachChip.tsx:34-40). But it only ever shows ONE tiny nudge chip. BOLD MOVE: turn those same signals into a real top-of-home "Morning brief" card that, in one glance, says e.g. "3 things due this week, 2 quicks to sort, nothing planned for today, want me to plan it?" with one-tap actions (auto-assign overdue items to today, accept-all predicted quicks). It reuses CoachSignals + the planner's assign logic and finally delivers the 90-second promise as a single readable surface rather than a widget hunt. For Priya specifically, this is the "tell me the next step" she needs every single morning, not just on day one.

opportunities.title: Replace the silent four-step linear walkthrough gate with a persistent progress checklist she can return to
opportunities.rationale: The walkthroughs have no completed state by design (Onboarding.tsx:7-8) and the only persistent guidance after dismissal is the day-scoped HomeCoachChip, which shows ONE suggestion and hides for the rest of the day once dismissed (HomeCoachChip.tsx:54-66). So a user who exits a walkthrough midway (very likely for someone who stalls) gets no sense of what's done, what's left, or how to resume, just the same four cards or a vanishing chip. BOLD MOVE: add a lightweight, always-visible "Set up Bozz" progress checklist (Connect a calendar, Add a topic, Capture your first quick, Plan today) derived from real state (topicCount, feedCount, inboxCount, emailConnected, all already threaded into Onboarding/HomeCoachChip), with a satisfying tick + percent. It gives a non-techy user a clear, resumable map of next steps instead of an all-or-nothing walkthrough, and it celebrates progress, which keeps her coming back past day one.

opportunities.title: Smarter deadline + reminder layer so captured deadlines actually surface as reminders
opportunities.rationale: taskParser already extracts rich natural-language deadlines (today, tomorrow, "the 5th of June", "in two weeks", "end of month" — taskParser.ts:300-396) and stores them on items, and DailyPlanner flags overdue/due-soon (DailyPlannerView.tsx:287-323). But for Priya the loop is open: she captures "essay due Friday," it lands in a topic with a deadline, and then nothing proactively tells her on Thursday night or Friday morning. The value of capturing a deadline is the nudge, and that nudge never comes unless she happens to open the planner. BOLD MOVE: build a self-contained reminder surface that turns stored deadlines into timely, glanceable alerts, an at-a-glance "due today / due tomorrow / overdue" strip pinned to Home and Quicks, plus optional in-app toast on launch ("Essay is due today"). This closes the capture-to-reminder loop using data the app ALREADY parses and stores, and makes Bozz feel like it's quietly looking out for her, which is exactly what a stall-prone user needs.

opportunities.title: Turn Ctrl+K into a real command palette with actions, not just a section-jumper
opportunities.area: Search / command palette (src/components/SearchModal.tsx, src/lib/search.ts, Dashboard.tsx Ctrl+K handler)
opportunities.rationale: Today Ctrl+K only navigates: choose() just calls onJump(section) (SearchModal.tsx:41-45), and the index (search.ts:25-55) doesn't even contain topics or topic tasks - it only has budget tx, inbox, calendar events and a hardcoded settings string list. For a keyboard-first user this is the single biggest miss: I can't fuzz-jump to a topic by name, can't find a task, and can't DO anything from the palette. Bold redesign: make the palette the app's keyboard spine. (1) Index every topic, every topic task (with topic+stage+deadline as sub), and every section so fuzzy search actually finds my stuff. (2) Add an ACTION layer - typed verbs run commands without leaving the keyboard: 'add <text> #topic ^friday' creates a task via the existing parseVoiceTasks/predictTopic/extractDeadline pipeline (taskParser.ts already does all the NLP), 'done <task>' advances a task to its done stage, 'go <topic>' jumps, 'theme dark', 'plan today'. (3) Results grouped Actions > Tasks > Topics > Calendar > Budget. Everything needed (Fuse, the parser, the topic mutators addTopicItem/onAdvanceStage in Dashboard.tsx) already exists - this is wiring, not new infra, and it transforms the product's feel for power users.

opportunities.title: A keyboard-driven command syntax + inline parse-preview for capture (#topic ^date !flag)
opportunities.rationale: The offline NLP engine (taskParser.ts: splitIntoTasks, predictTopic, extractDeadline) is genuinely excellent and is the app's hidden superpower - but the UI buries it behind dropdowns and date-pickers. In QuickAddModal a power-user types a thought then has to mouse over to a ChoicePicker and DatePicker per card (QuickAddModal.tsx:439-457). Bold change: add explicit power-syntax that the user types and SEES parsed live: '#music' or '#mus' pins/overrides the predicted topic (today prediction is implicit only), '^friday' / '^5 jun' sets the deadline inline, '!' or '!!' sets priority. Show a live token-highlighted preview (chips light up as you type, like Things/Todoist quick-entry) and let Enter commit straight to the topic - no review step, no mouse. This makes capture as fast as typing one line and removes the dropdown tax entirely while reusing the parser that's already shipped.

opportunities.title: A unified, dense, filterable 'All Tasks' workspace across every topic
opportunities.area: New cross-topic task view (data already in topics[].items; today only TodayWidget/DailyPlannerView aggregate, read-mostly)
opportunities.rationale: Tasks are siloed per topic inside TopicTodosWidget - there is no place to see and WORK all my tasks at once. The only aggregations are the read-only TodayWidget priorities and the manual 4-day DailyPlanner. With 8-10 topics, a power-user has no dense list to triage, filter (by topic/stage/deadline/overdue), sort, and bulk-act on. Bold proposal: a full-width 'Tasks' section that flattens every topic's items into one virtualised, high-density table - columns for stage chip, text (inline-edit), topic, deadline - with keyboard nav (j/k to move, e to edit, x to select, Enter to advance stage, d to set date), saved filters/views ('Overdue', 'Due this week', 'No deadline'), and multi-select bulk operations (advance stage, set deadline, move topic, schedule to today). All the mutators (addTopicItem, onAdvanceStage, updateItem) and deadline/priority logic (TodayWidget priorities(), deadlineEntries) already exist - this is an aggregation + interaction layer over existing state, no schema change.

opportunities.title: Global keyboard navigation: chord/number jumps, j/k lists, and a discoverable shortcut sheet
opportunities.area: App-wide keybindings (Dashboard.tsx keydown handler at 621-637; nav at 1098-1235; InboxView row Enter at 62-70)
opportunities.rationale: The entire app has exactly two shortcuts (Ctrl+K, Ctrl+B per Dashboard.tsx:623,627). Everything else - switching topics/sections, triaging Quicks, advancing stages - is mouse-only. The one row-level Enter in InboxView (62-70) only fires if a non-input control is focused, so even that is unreliable. For a keyboard-first user this is the dealbreaker. Bold but bounded redesign: (1) 'g then <key>' chords to jump (g h home, g i inbox, g c calendar, g t opens a topic quick-switcher), or Alt+1..9 for the first N sidebar items. (2) j/k + Enter triage in Quicks with the focused row auto-advancing after filing, plus number keys to pick a destination. (3) '?' opens a shortcut cheat-sheet overlay so the system is discoverable. This is self-contained React (a keymap module + focus management) and makes the app feel like a tool built for speed rather than a click-through dashboard.

opportunities.title: Recurring tasks + snooze: real task automation, currently impossible
opportunities.area: Task model + recurrence engine (src/lib/types.ts TopicItem at 21-33 has no recurrence; only budget RecurringItem exists at 167)
opportunities.rationale: TopicItem has deadline/dueMin but NO recurrence field - recurrence exists solely for budget (RecurringItem, types.ts:167). So 'submit weekly report every Monday', 'water plants daily', 'pay rent monthly' must be hand-recreated every cycle. For a power-user who wants automation, that's a glaring hole. Bold addition: add an optional recurrence rule to TopicItem (daily / weekdays / weekly-on-X / monthly), parseable from capture ('every monday', 'daily' - extractDeadline already understands weekday/relative phrases so the grammar is half-built). When a recurring item is advanced into its done stage, auto-spawn the next occurrence with the next deadline. Pair it with a 'snooze' action (push deadline to tomorrow / next week / pick) surfaced in the command palette and on each row. This is the kind of set-and-forget automation that earns daily-driver loyalty, and it's a localized model+helper change with no backend/schema-service impact (it lives in the synced user_data blob like everything else).

opportunities.title: Density mode + compact UI for power-users drowning in whitespace
opportunities.area: Appearance/layout (AppearancePrefs; widget rows e.g. TopicTodosWidget.tsx, InboxView.tsx, registry default sizes)
opportunities.rationale: Every list row is generous: TopicTodosWidget rows, InboxView rows (0.7rem 1rem padding), big DatePicker/ChoicePicker controls, and a one-stage-advance-per-click model. The whole UI is tuned for a calm first-timer, not for someone managing a lot of items on one screen. Bold but lower-risk move: a 'Compact / Density' appearance setting that tightens row padding, shrinks font/line-height, hides decorative chrome, and raises default widget item counts (TodayWidget caps at MAX_PRIORITIES=5, MAX_TASKS=5 - let dense users see 10-15). Also add a compact single-key stage cycle on rows. Reuses the existing appearance pipeline (applyAppearanceVars / AppearancePrefs) so it's a CSS-variable + a few caps change. It is the lowest-boldness item here, but it directly serves the 'I want to see and act on a lot, fast' power-user need and compounds the value of the denser views above.

opportunities.title: The 90-Second Morning Brief: a swipeable, time-boxed launch sequence (not a dashboard)
opportunities.rationale: The north star is literally "your morning in 90 seconds," but today HomeView.tsx delivers it as a static widget grid plus a greeting() string ('good morning. here is your day.'). Every competitor IS a dashboard or a list (Notion = database, Sunsama/Akiflow = day-plan, Things = lists). Nobody owns the morning RITUAL. Build a full-screen, swipeable card sequence that plays once per day on first open: Card 1 weather + first class/event from todayEvents, Card 2 today's deadlines (UpcomingDeadlinesWidget data), Card 3 'pick your top 3' pulled from DailyPlannerView assignments, Card 4 a single habit nudge (HabitsWidget), Card 5 a literal 90-second countdown that ends on a 'Start your day' button dropping you into Home. All data already exists in WidgetCtx; this is pure composition. This makes the tagline a felt experience, not a label, and is the kind of opening-30-seconds magic that sells a student app by word of mouth. Bold because it reframes the product from 'a dashboard you configure' to 'a daily ritual that runs you,' which no rival does.

opportunities.title: Talk-to-Bozz brain dump: ramble for 30s, get a triaged plan back
opportunities.rationale: Bozz already has the single best asset in this space and is under-using it. voiceRouter.ts + taskParser.ts already split a multi-clause ramble into separate tasks, predict the right topic per task, AND extract deadlines, all offline with zero API cost (see splitIntoTasks, parseVoiceTasks, predictTopic). Notion AI, Sunsama, and Akiflow all charge monthly for inferior cloud AI. The current QuickCapture window (Ctrl+B) is a tiny one-line box that mostly dumps to inbox. Turn it into a hero 'brain dump' surface: a big mic, a live-growing list of parsed task chips as you speak ('book hotel → Travel · tomorrow', 'finish essay → Uni · Friday'), each editable, then one tap files them all to their predicted topics and the planner. Students think in rambles before exams; this is the exact moment of overwhelm Bozz can own. Lean into the marketing line 'no AI subscription, works on a plane.' Bold because it weaponises an already-built capability into the product's signature verb.

opportunities.title: Now / Next focus mode: collapse the whole app to the one thing to do right now
opportunities.rationale: Things and Akiflow give you a list; Sunsama gives you a day; none answer 'what do I do in the next 25 minutes RIGHT NOW.' Bozz has the pieces scattered: PomodoroWidget.tsx (a focus timer), DailyPlannerView (today's assigned items with stages), and todayEvents. Fuse them into a single-task 'Now' mode: full-screen, dimmed, shows ONLY the top item from today's plan (next deadline / next planned task), a 25-min Pomodoro wired to it, and a 'Next' preview of the one after. Completing the timer auto-advances the topic stage (advanceStage logic already exists in DailyPlannerView) and surfaces the next task. This is the ADHD/exam-week killer feature: zero choice paralysis. Bold because it deliberately HIDES the dashboard, the opposite of what every productivity app does, and that restraint is the differentiator for stressed students.

opportunities.title: Streak & momentum layer that gamifies finishing, with a weekly 'wrapped' card
opportunities.rationale: Students are the Duolingo/BeReal generation; productivity for them is driven by streaks and shareable moments, which Notion/Things/Akiflow pointedly avoid (they target professionals). Bozz already tracks everything needed: Habit.entries already store per-day completion (streak math is trivial), TopicItem.completedAt timestamps every finished task, and review.ts already computes weeklyStats (spend, tasks done by topic, completed-this-week per topic in ReviewView.tsx). Build (a) a momentum streak on the home / morning brief ('5 days planned in a row'), and (b) turn the existing Weekly Review into a Spotify-Wrapped-style shareable card: 'This week: 23 tasks done, 4 deadlines beaten, longest focus streak 6 days, top topic Uni.' Export as an image users post to their group chat, free organic acquisition. Bold because it makes a serious planner emotionally sticky and viral, a lane no competitor in this category will touch.

opportunities.title: Deadline pressure radar: auto-surface what's about to blow up before you ask
opportunities.rationale: Every rival is reactive, you must go look at your list. Bozz can be proactive cheaply: TopicItem already carries deadline + completedAt + stageId, and topics carry kanban stages, so the app knows which items are due soon AND still stuck in an early stage (i.e. not started). Build a 'Radar' that ranks open items by a pressure score = closeness-of-deadline x how-far-from-done (stage index). Surface the top 1-2 as a calm, single-line nudge on Home and in the Morning Brief: 'Essay due in 2 days and still in To-do.' This is purely local math over existing data, no AI, no network. It directly serves students who miss things not because they lack a list but because nothing tells them which list item is the fire. Bold because it turns Bozz from passive storage into something that has an opinion about your day, the trust-building moment that converts a tool into 'my app.'

opportunities.title: Shareable Worlds: student-made life-template packs (timetable + topic + look in one link)
opportunities.rationale: Worlds (WorldsView.tsx, lib/worlds.ts) already bundle a theme + topic pipeline + ambient sound + starter layout, and worldToTopic/worldToFolder already instantiate them. Right now they're a closed bundled set. Notion's entire growth engine is its template marketplace; no student planner has one. Let users EXPORT their topic+stages+keywords+look as a shareable World (serialise to a JSON blob / link, importable via the existing apply machinery, no backend needed for v1, just file/clipboard). A med student shares their 'Rotation Tracker' World; a law cohort shares 'Bar Prep.' This makes Bozz spread inside a class or society, the cheapest possible distribution. Bold because it bets the growth strategy on user-generated content built entirely on infrastructure that already exists, turning a cosmetic feature into a moat.

priorities.title: Wire the NL parser into typed quick-add (capture = triage = plan in one keystroke)
priorities.why: Unanimous (6/6) and the lowest-effort/highest-leverage change in the deck. Verified at HEAD: QuickAddWidget.tsx:19 still calls addToInbox(v, deadline) with the raw string, so typing 'essay due friday for bio' is stored as literal text with no date and no topic, while QuickAddModal.tsx:89 ALREADY runs parseVoiceTasks (extractDeadline + predictTopic) on typed input with a live preview. The widget context (context.ts) already exposes topics, addTopicItem, addToInbox, and addDeadline, so this is pure reuse of shipped, tested logic with zero new infra and zero schema change. Run extractDeadline + predictTopic in submit(), show an inline confirm chip ('essay - Bio - Fri'), auto-route to the predicted topic on high confidence (skipping Quicks), and on a zero-topic account infer a domain via the existing DOMAIN_KEYWORDS map (predictTopic returns null at topics.length===0) to offer a one-tap 'Create Bio topic and file this here' chip. This fires the product's best hidden asset on the first keystroke and first capture, and a dated task then auto-surfaces in TodayWidget's dueRows with zero planner ceremony. Land it first, green, as the foundation for priorities 2 and 3.

priorities.title: Ship the zero-config 'Today' briefing as the DEFAULT landing surface (and collapse onboarding onto it)
priorities.why: The single most-cited move on the board (6/6) and the literal north-star reframe from 'a dashboard you configure' to 'a briefing that runs you.' Verified: DEFAULT_HOME is [] (registry.tsx:111) and TodayWidget.tsx already computes the entire 90-second brief (narrated SummaryLine, overdue/today/week priorities, timed events, dueRows, nextEvent) - it is just one optional card a user must discover and place. Promote it to a full-width zero-config landing surface and demote the customizable react-grid to an optional 'Board' for power users, gated behind a default-section flag so returning users' saved homeLayout is untouched (hard constraint). Critically, fix the first-run: Onboarding.tsx:324 still gates new accounts behind 'Four short walkthroughs ... Do them in order' that teach tool vocabulary ('A topic is an area of your life', SIDEBAR_STEPS line 125) instead of an outcome. Replace that gate with a 60-second outcome flow (paste/build timetable -> add your first 3 deadlines via the priority-1 NL quick-add -> here's your morning), route BOTH the cold-start and skip paths onto the populated briefing (cold-start seeding already exists in coldStart.ts/seedColdStart), and demote the spotlight tours to re-runnable 'learn more' in Settings. Pure composition over existing WidgetCtx state.

priorities.title: Make the timetable first-class: recurring class model + location + live 'next class' banner
priorities.why: The boldest justified model change of the round and 5/6 board consensus, including the CTO who ranks it the single most-defensible bold bet. This is the one feature onboarding explicitly sells ('your real classes fill Today') that the data model literally cannot hold today: verified CalendarNote (types.ts:282-293) is single-day-only with no recurrence and no location field, so a student can paste a read-only ICS but cannot build 'BIO101, Tue 14:00, Room 3.21, every week.' Add a recurring CalendarNote variant (weekday + start/end + room/location) expanded CLIENT-SIDE in lib/calendar.ts the exact same way topicDeadlineEvents() already synthesizes events (NOT a parallel store - this directly answers the CTO's data-model-sprawl concern), add an optional location field to CalendarEvent surfaced in TodayWidget, and add a persistent 'Next: BIO101 - 14:00 - Room 3.21 - in 18 min' banner driven off the nextEvent TodayWidget already derives. Backward-compatible optional fields, no migration, no sync-blob bloat, touches no auth/payments/schema/sync. This converts Bozz from a glorified ICS viewer into the one thing a student checks before leaving the house. Note: the CTO's grades/Modules layer and the entire freelancer/multi-client cluster were explicitly deferred by the COO this round to protect the north star and avoid model sprawl - hold them for a future round.

experiments.priority: Wire the NL parser into typed quick-add (capture = triage = plan in one keystroke)

variants.name: A. Inline confirm chip (preview-then-commit)
variants.approach: Smallest, safest reuse. ONLY file changed: src/components/widgets/QuickAddWidget.tsx. In submit(), replace the raw addToInbox(v, deadline) with const parsed = parseVoiceTasks(v, topics); take parsed[0] (the widget is single-line, so one task). Render an inline confirm chip BELOW the input showing cleaned text + predicted topic dot/name + relative date, e.g. 'essay - Bio - Fri', driven by a debounced live preview off onChange (mirror QuickAddModal.tsx:123-133's 320ms parseTimer). Enter commits by destination: if parsed.topicId, call ctx.addTopicItem(topicId, text, deadline); else if parsed.deadline, call ctx.addDeadline(text, deadline) (already routes/buckets); else ctx.addToInbox(text, deadline). The manual DatePicker value overrides the parsed deadline (manual wins, matching QuickAddModal.tsx:182-184). The hardcoded 'added to Quicks' flash becomes destination-aware ('filed in Bio' / 'added to Quicks'). No new exports, no Dashboard changes, no confidence math - the user always sees and confirms routing via the chip before Enter. Green foundation: every call target already exists on ctx so TypeScript stays clean.

variants.name: B. One-keystroke auto-route with confidence + undo
variants.approach: Bolder: Enter does the whole thing with no confirm step, auto-routing to a topic ONLY when confidence is high, else degrading to Quicks. Files: src/lib/taskParser.ts and src/components/widgets/QuickAddWidget.tsx. Because predictTopic (taskParser.ts:262) returns only Topic | null with no score, add ONE exported helper: export function predictTopicScored(text, topics): { topic: Topic; score: number } | null - lift the existing private topicScore loop (lines 262-271) into it and have predictTopic call it (behavior preserved). submit() then parses and, if score >= a threshold (e.g. >=4, the weight of one exact name-word match) AND a topic was found, calls ctx.addTopicItem and SKIPS Quicks entirely so 'essay due friday for bio' lands as a dated task directly in Bio and auto-surfaces in TodayWidget's dueRows. If a deadline was parsed but no confident topic, call ctx.addDeadline; otherwise ctx.addToInbox. Show a 2-3s post-commit toast with the destination plus an inline 'Undo / send to Quicks instead' action (stash the last-committed payload in a ref; undo removes from the topic and pushes to inbox via addToInbox) so confident auto-routing is never a black box. Truest expression of 'capture = triage = plan in one keystroke' for users who already have topics.

variants.name: C. Zero-topic domain-inference 'Create & file' chip
variants.approach: Most ambitious; targets the deck's call-out that predictTopic returns null when topics.length===0, so a fresh account gets zero magic on its first capture. Builds on Variant A's inline chip. Files: src/lib/taskParser.ts (export DOMAIN_KEYWORDS, currently module-private at line 190, and add export function inferDomain(text): string | null that scores text against those keyword arrays and returns the best domain key, e.g. essay/study -> study, guitar -> music); src/components/widgets/context.ts (add createTopicAndAdd(domainName, text, deadline) to WidgetCtx); src/components/Dashboard.tsx (implement createTopicAndAdd by reusing makeBlankTopic + the single-setTopics-updater pattern already proven in addDeadline at Dashboard.tsx:742-770, so create+append never races, and wire it into both ctx call sites at lines 1534 and 1671); src/components/widgets/QuickAddWidget.tsx (chip + tap handler). When the parse yields no topic AND the account has no matching/zero topics, the chip reads 'Create Study topic and file this here'; one tap atomically creates a real topic (name = Title-cased domain, sensible icon/keywords) and files the dated item. Turns the very first capture on a new account into an instant, populated topic - the strongest demo of the app's hidden asset - while still falling through to addToInbox if the chip is dismissed.

votes.why: As a first-year student, my make-or-break moment with Bozz is the first five minutes, and on day one I have zero topics. I verified the gap the variant targets: predictTopic returns null when topics.length===0 (taskParser.ts:262-263), and addToInbox only attaches a suggested topic via that same predictTopic (Dashboard.tsx:727-732). That means Variants A and B both reduce to a plain 'added to Quicks' on a fresh account, no topic dot, no routing, no magic, exactly when I most need to be impressed. They're built for the power-user who already curated topics with keywords (B's auto-route is genuinely the dream in week three), but they leave my first capture flat. Variant C is the only one that fixes the empty-account experience: I type 'essay due friday', the chip offers 'Create Study topic and file this here', one tap spins up a real populated topic with a dated task that immediately surfaces in Today. It turns an empty dashboard into a living one from keystroke one, the strongest possible demo of capture=triage=plan. Crucially it builds on A's confirm chip, so I still see and approve the destination before committing (not a black box), and it falls through to Quicks if I dismiss, so nothing is forced. The risk is that it touches the most files (taskParser.ts, context.ts, Dashboard.tsx, QuickAddWidget.tsx), but I confirmed the Dashboard side can safely reuse the proven single-setTopics-updater create+append pattern already shipped in addDeadline (Dashboard.tsx:742-770, including makeBlankTopic + lazy-create), so the race-free foundation already exists and TypeScript can stay green. Bold over timid, and it serves the actual primary user on the actual hardest moment.

Speaking as Tom, a freelancer: the whole reason I'd use the typed quick-add widget instead of the mic modal is that I'm mid-flow and want zero ceremony. What sells me on A over B and C is that it makes the routing visible BEFORE I commit, without making me babysit anything afterward. I verified the chip is honest: parseVoiceTasks already returns text + topicId + topicName/color + deadlineLabel (taskParser.ts:16-24, 405-431), so the chip can show exactly the cleaned text, the topic dot, and the relative date that Enter will use, driven off the same 320ms debounced preview the modal already runs (QuickAddModal.tsx:123-133). I get a live 'essay - Bio - Fri' read-out and the manual DatePicker still wins (matches QuickAddModal.tsx:182-184), so I'm never surprised. As a freelancer my inputs are messy and varied (client calls, invoices, gym, groceries), and a keyword scorer with no score exposed is going to misfire often; the offline DOMAIN_KEYWORDS/topicScore heuristic (taskParser.ts:190-254) is genuinely coarse. B's silent auto-route-on-confidence trades my trust for one fewer glance, and its undo-toast is a worse safety net than just not having filed it wrong in the first place: an Undo I have 2-3 seconds to notice while I'm already typing the next thing is exactly the kind of thing I'd miss, and then a task is buried in the wrong topic. C is the most seductive on a brand-new account, but I'm a freelancer who already has my topics set up, so its headline win (zero-topic domain inference) is a cold-start feature I'd basically never hit, while it spends real risk: it widens the public API of taskParser.ts, adds createTopicAndAdd to WidgetCtx, and touches Dashboard.tsx at two ctx call sites (1534 and 1671, both confirmed) plus a new setTopics updater. That is a lot of surface area, and a tap that silently spawns a whole new topic from a guessed domain is the single change most likely to clutter my sidebar with junk topics I didn't want. Crucially, A is the only variant scoped to ONE file (QuickAddWidget.tsx) with every call target (addTopicItem, addDeadline, addToInbox) already living on ctx and verified wired at Dashboard.tsx:1534, so TypeScript stays green with no new exports and no Dashboard edits, and it delivers the actual headline promise today: my typed line gets cleaned, dated, and filed into the right topic in one Enter, and a dated topic item immediately shows up in the home Today widget's dueRows (TodayWidget.tsx:72,299-311, deadlineEntries). It's the version of 'capture = triage = plan in one keystroke' I'd actually trust enough to use every day, and it's the clean foundation B's confidence/undo and C's create-and-file can be layered onto later once the chip has proven the routing is right.

votes.preferred: B. One-keystroke auto-route with confidence + undo
votes.why:
As Sam (a power user who already has topics set up), B is the only variant that actually delivers the stated goal "capture = triage = plan in ONE keystroke." I type "essay due friday for bio", press Enter, and it lands as a dated task directly in Bio and auto-surfaces in TodayWidget's dueRows. No chip to read, no second tap. A and C both reintroduce a confirm step (A's preview-then-commit chip, C's "Create & file" tap), which is exactly the timid friction the dogfood-v2 AGGRESSIVE brief tells us to avoid, and it makes Enter a two-cognitive-step action rather than one keystroke.

B is also well-grounded in the code I read. The baseline already half-does this: addToInbox (Dashboard.tsx:728) computes predictTopic and stashes suggestedTopicId, but the item just sits in Quicks awaiting manual triage, so the "plan" step never happens. addDeadline (Dashboard.tsx:742-771) is the proven workhorse B leans on: it predicts a topic, files a dated item, and lazily buckets on zero-topic accounts, all in one safe setTopics updater. B's threshold is principled against the real scoring weights in taskParser.ts: topicScore gives +4 for one exact name-word match (line 217), +6 for a user keyword (line 221), so a >=4 gate genuinely means "at least as strong as a confident name hit." The needed predictTopicScored helper is a clean lift of the existing private topicScore loop with predictTopic delegating to it, so behavior is preserved and TypeScript stays green; every commit target (ctx.addTopicItem, ctx.addDeadline, ctx.addToInbox) already exists on WidgetCtx (context.ts:17-23), so it is self-contained across just two files (src/lib/taskParser.ts + QuickAddWidget.tsx) and touches none of the hard-constraint areas.

My one reservation as a power user is that auto-routing on a >=4 score will occasionally misfile, and aggressive auto-routing could feel like a black box. But B's design answers exactly that: it routes to a topic ONLY at high confidence, degrades to addDeadline when there is a date but no confident topic, and falls through to addToInbox otherwise, so a capture is never lost; and the 2-3s post-commit toast with an inline "Undo / send to Quicks instead" (stashing the last payload in a ref) is the right escape hatch, far better than forcing me to confirm every single time. That is the speed/control trade a power user wants.

I rejected C despite it being the most ambitious because it optimizes the cold-start, zero-topic first-capture case, which is not my situation as an established user; its domain-inference "Create Study topic and file this here" chip is a strong new-account demo but still a confirm-tap, and it spreads across four files including Dashboard.tsx and context.ts for more surface and risk. I rejected A because it is the safest but the least faithful to the goal: a debounced live-preview chip plus preview-then-commit is polish, not the one-keystroke triage I actually want.

experiments.priority: Ship the zero-config 'Today' briefing as the DEFAULT landing surface (and collapse onboarding onto it)

variants.name: Briefing-First Shell (Today as a fixed hero, Board demoted to a tab)
variants.approach: Restructure the Home section into a two-layer surface: a permanent full-width 'Today' briefing hero at the top, and the existing react-grid demoted to an optional 'Board' below a segmented toggle ('Briefing' | 'Board'). CONCRETE WIRING: In Dashboard.tsx (the activeSection==='home' block, lines 1509-1541) render, above HomeView, a new <TodayHero ctx={...}/> that reuses TodayWidget's exact computation by extracting the pure helpers (priorities(), SummaryLine, PrioritiesSection, EventsSection, TasksSection already live in TodayWidget.tsx) into a shared module and rendering them full-width with larger type (the widget is already a self-contained reader of WidgetCtx, so this is pure recomposition, no new data). The DeadlineQuickAdd NL input is promoted to sit directly under the SummaryLine as the hero's primary action. A small segmented control switches a local `homeView` state ('briefing' default) between the hero and the existing <HomeView> grid; when 'board' is empty it shows today's HomeEmptyState/starter templates unchanged. RETURNING-USER SAFETY: nothing reads or writes homeLayout differently. A new appearance flag `homeSurface?: 'briefing' | 'board'` defaults to 'briefing' for everyone but is auto-set to 'board' once at load for any account whose loaded homeLayout is non-empty (mirrors the existing defaultSection migration at Dashboard.tsx:302-305), so power users with a saved board keep landing on their board untouched while new/empty accounts get the briefing. FIRST-RUN: the briefing IS the cold-start landing target, so finishWelcome (Dashboard.tsx:373) needs no route change. The greeting() line already in HomeView moves to the hero. This is the most literal execution of 'briefing that runs you, board for power users' with the smallest behavioral surprise.

variants.name: Outcome Onboarding -> Live Briefing (rip out the vocabulary gate, keep one surface)
variants.approach: Keep a single Home surface but make the FIRST RUN produce a populated briefing in ~60 seconds and delete the tool-vocabulary gate. CONCRETE WIRING: (1) Replace the welcome sequence's framing so it reads as an outcome, not a tour. The 3-phase overlay (theme -> coldstart -> timetable) in Dashboard.tsx already does exactly the right shape; relabel coldstart copy to outcome language and ADD a fourth lightweight phase `welcomePhase==='deadlines'` between timetable and finish: a full-screen step reusing the existing parseVoiceTasks + addDeadline path (the same logic DeadlineQuickAdd uses) with three stacked NL inputs prefilled with placeholders ('e.g. stats essay due friday 5pm'), then finishWelcome lands them on the now-populated Today. (2) DELETE the vocabulary gate: in Onboarding.tsx remove the 'Getting started / Four short walkthroughs / Do them in order' card (lines 315-340) from the home render path entirely; move the four WalkCards verbatim into a re-runnable 'Learn more' panel in Settings (replayWalkthroughs at Dashboard.tsx:392 already exists and already navigates home + force-shows; repoint it to open Settings' learn-more list instead). The SpotlightOverlay engine and FLOW_STEPS stay 100% intact, just invoked from Settings. (3) Make Today the de-facto home for new accounts by giving DEFAULT_HOME a single full-width 'today' item (registry.tsx:111) instead of [] — returning users are unaffected because a stored homeLayout always overrides DEFAULT_HOME (already true, see the comment at registry.tsx:107-110 and the load path). Lowest-risk variant: no new shell component, reuses the welcome overlay machinery, and the only structural deletion is the onboarding card that everyone agrees teaches vocabulary instead of outcome.

variants.name: Always-On Briefing Banner (zero structural change to the grid, briefing as a sticky strip)
variants.approach: Treat the briefing as an ambient, always-present strip rather than replacing or tabbing the grid — the most additive, lowest-blast-radius option. CONCRETE WIRING: Render a condensed, full-width 'BriefingStrip' permanently at the top of the Home section (Dashboard.tsx home block, above HomeView) that shows only the highest-signal parts of TodayWidget: the narrated SummaryLine + top-3 PrioritiesSection rows + nextEvent + the inline DeadlineQuickAdd, all reusing the extracted helpers from TodayWidget.tsx (no new data, pure read of WidgetCtx). Below it, the existing grid renders exactly as today — so a power user's saved board is literally untouched (no migration, no flag), and a new user with an empty grid sees the briefing strip ON TOP of the existing HomeEmptyState (which keeps offering starter templates). The strip is collapsible via an appearance flag `briefingStrip?: boolean` (default true) toggled from Settings, so power users who want a pure board can hide it.
