FIRST-RUN: fold onboarding INTO the strip — when topics.length===0 or calendarFeeds.length===0, the strip swaps its right side for an inline outcome checklist ('1. Paste your timetable  2. Add your first 3 deadlines  3. Here's your morning') wired to the same routes HomeCoachChip uses (HomeCoachChip.tsx:34-39 already encodes this exact signal ranking — reuse pickSuggestion). The 'Getting started' vocabulary card (Onboarding.tsx:315-340) is removed from home and the four walkthroughs become re-runnable from Settings via the existing replayWalkthroughs handler. This variant ships the briefing as the dominant landing element WITHOUT a tab/shell rebuild, so it's the fastest to land and the safest for returning users, at the cost of the briefing being a strip rather than a full hero surface.

experiments.winner: Outcome Onboarding -> Live Briefing (rip out the vocabulary gate, keep one surface)

votes.preferred: Outcome Onboarding -> Live Briefing (rip out the vocabulary gate, keep one surface)
votes.why:
As Maya, my real round-1 pain isn't the shape of the home surface, it's that the FIRST thing I meet is a "Getting started: four short walkthroughs, do them in order" card (Onboarding.tsx:315-340) that teaches me tool vocabulary (sidebar, topic page, connect, quicks) before I get anything back. It feels like homework. And even after the welcome flow, my Today is empty: I verified seedColdStart (coldStart.ts:114-124) deliberately seeds NO deadlines, so on day one priorities() returns nothing and the briefing reads "nothing pressing" — which is hollow, not magic.

Variant 2 is the only one that fixes BOTH. It (a) deletes the vocabulary gate from the home path and relocates the four WalkCards into a re-runnable Settings panel with the SpotlightOverlay/FLOW_STEPS engine 100% intact (replayWalkthroughs at Dashboard.tsx:392 already navigates home + force-shows, so it's a small repoint), and (b) adds a fourth welcome phase between timetable and finish that has me type my first 3 deadlines through the exact parseVoiceTasks + addDeadline path DeadlineQuickAdd already uses — so finishWelcome (Dashboard.tsx:373) lands me on a Today that is actually populated with MY priorities. That is literally "your morning in 90 seconds" delivered on the first run, which is the north star.

Why not the others: Variant 1 (Briefing-First Shell) builds a full hero + segmented toggle + a new homeSurface migration flag, but a hero amplifies the empty-Today problem on day one (a giant blank hero is worse than a small blank widget), and TodayWidget's type is sized as a tiny grid tile (0.72-0.78rem), so "render full-width with larger type" is more work and visual risk than it sounds. Variant 3 (sticky BriefingStrip) is the timid, additive choice that demotes the briefing to a garnish strip ON TOP of the existing grid — it directly contradicts the goal of making Today the DEFAULT landing surface and the v2 be-bold mandate. Variant 2 is simultaneously the boldest in outcome (kills the gate, guarantees a live briefing) and the lowest structural risk: no new shell component, reuses the welcome overlay machinery, and returning users are untouched because a stored homeLayout always overrides DEFAULT_HOME (confirmed at registry.tsx:107-111).

votes.preferred: Outcome Onboarding -> Live Briefing (rip out the vocabulary gate, keep one surface)
votes.why:
As Tom, a freelancer, my actual problem on day one isn't "which surface do I land on" — it's that the current first run hands me a blank home plus a "Four short walkthroughs, do them in order" homework card (Onboarding.tsx:322-338) that teaches me Bozz's vocabulary (sidebar/topics/stages/widgets) before it has ever shown me a single useful thing. That card is the real gate between me and "my morning in 90 seconds," and this is the only variant that deletes it outright and replaces it with an outcome: by finishWelcome I have a Today that is actually populated with my deadlines, not an empty grid waiting on me to learn the tool.

I verified the wiring this variant leans on is real and low-risk, which is why I trust it to ship without breaking my account: DEFAULT_HOME is genuinely [] with an explicit comment that a stored homeLayout overrides it (registry.tsx:107-111) and the load path proves it (Dashboard.tsx:289), so flipping DEFAULT_HOME to a single full-width 'today' item changes nothing for returning users — exactly the safety it claims. The welcome overlay already has the theme->coldstart->timetable phase machine (welcomePhase at Dashboard.tsx:121, rendered 1473-1477), so adding a 'deadlines' phase that reuses the same parseVoiceTasks/addDeadline path DeadlineQuickAdd already uses is a true extension of a proven pattern, not a new subsystem. And replayWalkthroughs (Dashboard.tsx:392-399) plus the intact SpotlightOverlay/FLOW_STEPS engine means the walkthroughs aren't lost — they move to a re-runnable Settings panel for when I actually want to learn, instead of being forced on me cold.

I rejected the other two on grounds that matter to me specifically. Briefing-First Shell is the most behaviorally invasive and is the only variant that has to invent a brand-new appearance flag (homeSurface) plus a one-time auto-set-once migration; it justifies that by claiming it "mirrors the existing defaultSection migration at Dashboard.tsx:302-305," but that block is actually just a hidden-section fallback guard (lines 301-306), not a homeLayout-driven migration — so the precedent it relies on doesn't really exist, and a migration that mislabels a saved board as 'briefing' or vice versa is exactly the kind of first-impression bug that would make me distrust the app. The Always-On Briefing Banner is the safest but the weakest: a condensed strip sitting ON TOP of an otherwise-unchanged empty HomeEmptyState means a brand-new freelancer still sees a half-empty home and still has to do the setup work — it ships the briefing as decoration over the same cold start rather than fixing the cold start. The whole stated goal is to make the zero-config briefing the DEFAULT and collapse onboarding onto it; variant 2 is the one that genuinely populates the briefing on first run and removes the vocabulary onboarding, with the smallest structural deletion (one card) and no new flag or shell component to get wrong.

votes.preferred: Outcome Onboarding -> Live Briefing (rip out the vocabulary gate, keep one surface)
votes.why:
As Sam (power user with a saved board), this is the only variant that fully delivers the goal AND touches me by zero. Verified against the code: a stored homeLayout already overrides DEFAULT_HOME (homeItems init = loadedHomeItems ?? DEFAULT_HOME, and registry.tsx:107-110 says so), and loadedHomeItems is only set when valid.length>0 (registry load lines 195/201). So flipping DEFAULT_HOME from [] to a single full-width 'today' (registry.tsx:111) gives new/empty accounts a populated briefing as the de-facto home while my saved board renders byte-for-byte unchanged. That is exactly where Variant 1 (Briefing-First Shell) loses me: it adds a brand-new homeSurface flag + TodayHero component + segmented toggle + a one-time auto-migration that flips returning users to 'board' (mirroring defaultSection at Dashboard.tsx:302-305). That is a shell rebuild plus a migration that can misclassify any account whose board didn't load as non-empty for a transient reason, and it demotes my grid behind a tab. Bigger blast radius for the same outcome. Variant 3 (sticky strip) is safe but explicitly fails the goal: it ships the briefing as a strip, not 'the DEFAULT landing surface,' and permanently eats vertical space above every power user's board until they find a Settings toggle.

Variant 2 also nails the second half of the goal (collapse onboarding). The vocabulary gate is real and prominent: Onboarding.tsx:315-340 is a full card titled 'Getting started / Four short walkthroughs / Do them in order' - tour framing, not outcome. Deleting it from the home render path while moving the four WalkCards into a re-runnable Settings panel via the existing replayWalkthroughs handler (Dashboard.tsx:392-399) keeps the SpotlightOverlay/useWalkEngine machinery 100% intact - nothing valuable is thrown away, just relocated. The added welcomePhase==='deadlines' step reuses real existing paths (addDeadline + the DeadlineQuickAdd parse logic; the overlay already has the theme->coldstart->timetable shape at Dashboard.tsx:1473-1484), and finishWelcome (373) already lands on home, so no route change. Net: lowest-risk, no new shell component, only structural deletion is the card everyone agrees teaches vocabulary instead of outcome - and as a power user I keep my board exactly as it is.

experiments.priority: Make the timetable first-class: recurring class model + location + live 'next class' banner

variants.name: Recurring-CalendarNote (the minimal, CTO-aligned expansion)
variants.approach:
Extend the EXISTING CalendarNote shape with backward-compatible optional fields and expand recurrence purely client-side, exactly mirroring how topicDeadlineEvents() already synthesizes events. NO new store, NO migration.

DATA MODEL (src/lib/types.ts:282-293): add to CalendarNote three optional fields: `repeatDay?: number` (0-6 weekday; presence = this note is a recurring class template rather than a one-off), `location?: string`, and an optional bound `repeatUntil?: number` (ms; defaults to ~16 weeks / end of term if absent). Add `location?: string` to CalendarEvent (src/lib/types.ts:295-308). All optional, so existing stored notes and ICS feeds keep working untouched — no sync-blob bloat, no schema/sync changes.

EXPANSION (src/lib/calendar.ts): in noteEvents(), branch on `repeatDay != null`. For a recurring note, instead of emitting one event at `n.date`, loop the visible horizon (today → repeatUntil, capped ~16 weeks) emitting one CalendarEvent per matching weekday: id=`note:${n.id}:${weekTs}`, start = thatDay + startMin*60000, carrying color/startMin/endMin/location and source:'note'. One-off notes keep their current single-event path. Because Dashboard.tsx (828-848) and CalendarView.tsx (734-736) already pipe noteEvents() through eventsOnDay(), every recurring instance automatically appears in Today, Upcoming, and the week/day grid with ZERO call-site changes — this is the whole point: one expander, every consumer.

UI: in NoteForm (CalendarView.tsx ~60-154) add a 'Repeats weekly' checkbox and a 'Room / location' text input. When 'Repeats weekly' is on, derive repeatDay from the picked date's getDay() and persist repeatDay+location. Surface `location` in TodayWidget EventsSection (after the title) and in the calendar grid event chips.

BANNER: a new <NextClassBanner> component fed by the SAME nextEvent TodayWidget already derives (TodayWidget.tsx:436-437), but lifted so it can render persistently at the top of the home grid (or as the TodayWidget's first row). It shows `Next: BIO101 · 14:00 · Room 3.21 · in 18 min` with a once-a-minute setInterval tick recomputing the countdown; collapses to nothing when no upcoming timed event today.

Why: smallest possible diff that fully satisfies the brief, directly answers the data-model-sprawl concern (recurrence is a pure projection, never stored expanded), and is provably backward compatible.

variants.name: Dedicated Class entity + timetable importer (the 'real timetable' power approach)
variants.approach:
Treat a class as a first-class concept distinct from a generic note, optimizing for the 'BIO101, Tue 14:00, Room 3.21, every week' mental model AND for getting a whole timetable in fast — without ever touching auth/payments/schema/sync.

DATA MODEL: introduce a new local-only array `classes: ClassBlock[]` persisted via the SAME idb/local pattern as calendarNotes (a new key in the existing local persistence list in Dashboard.tsx — purely additive client storage, no Supabase schema change). ClassBlock = { id, code (e.g. 'BIO101'), title?, day (0-6), startMin, endMin, location?, color, weeks?: number[] (optional teaching-week filter), termStart?, termEnd? }. This keeps generic CalendarNote untrenched and gives classes their own clean editor.

EXPANSION: a new `classEvents(classes, horizon)` in src/lib/calendar.ts produces CalendarEvent[] (source:'note' or a new 'class' source) exactly like topicDeadlineEvents/noteEvents — one event per occurring weekday in the horizon, carrying location + a code-prefixed title. Wire it into Dashboard.tsx todayEvents/upcomingEvents and CalendarView's merge alongside the existing synthesizers (3 one-line additions). Same projection-not-storage discipline as Variant A, just a separate typed entity.

KILLER UI — the Timetable editor: a dedicated 'My Timetable' screen presenting a Mon-Fri grid where the student drops class blocks, plus a fast 'add class' row (code, day dropdown, start/end, room). Crucially add a PASTE-TEXT importer: a textarea where a student pastes lines like `BIO101 Tue 14:00-15:00 Room 3.21` and a lightweight regex parser (in lib/calendar.ts, pure + unit-testable) turns each line into a ClassBlock, previewed before commit. This finally lets a student BUILD their timetable (the gap the brief calls out) — not just paste a read-only ICS — and seed an entire week in seconds.

BANNER: same persistent <NextClassBanner> as Variant A but it can label the source ('Next class') and, because ClassBlock has a clean code+room, render the richest version: `Next class · BIO101 · 14:00 · Room 3.21 · in 18 min`, with the countdown updating each minute.

Why: best-in-class for the actual job (own the timetable as a concept, batch-entry, dedicated grid) and most defensible long-term home for a future grades/Modules layer. Costs a new entity + screen — heavier than A, but the COO-deferred Modules layer would later hang naturally off ClassBlock.code.

variants.name: Live 'Now & Next' command bar (recurrence as a thin enabler, banner as the hero)
variants.approach:
Lead with the north-star moment ('your morning in 90 seconds' / the thing you check before leaving the house) and treat the recurring data model as the minimum substrate needed to power a genuinely live, always-on banner. Recurrence is implemented as the LIGHTEST possible version of Variant A; the ambition goes into the banner UX.

DATA MODEL: identical minimal additive change to Variant A — optional repeatDay + location on CalendarNote, optional location on CalendarEvent (src/lib/types.ts), recurrence expanded client-side in noteEvents() (src/lib/calendar.ts). Same backward-compat / no-migration / no-sync-bloat guarantees. NoteForm gains the 'Repeats weekly' + 'Room' fields.

HERO — the Now & Next bar: a persistent, full-width bar pinned at the top of the home view (above the widget grid), driven off a new `useNowAndNext(todayEvents)` hook that recomputes on a once-a-minute setInterval and on visibilitychange (so it's correct the instant the laptop is reopened). It renders THREE live states from the same event stream Today already has:
  • NOW: if an event is in progress → `Now: BIO101 · Room 3.21 · ends 15:00 (32 min left)` with a thin progress bar across the bar showing % elapsed.
  • NEXT: else → `Next: BIO101 · 14:00 · Room 3.21 · in 18 min`, the countdown going amber under ~10 min (reusing the existing WARNING color token).
  • CLEAR: else → `No more classes today — you're done` (the satisfying empty state).
Clicking the bar jumps to the calendar day view (setActiveSection('calendar')). Location is a tappable chip; if it looks like a room code it can deep-link the existing Map widget/section later (hook left as a no-op now to avoid scope creep).

TODAY WIDGET: still surfaces location inline in EventsSection, but the banner — not the widget — becomes the primary 'before you leave the house' surface, which is the brief's stated goal.

Why: maximizes perceived value per line of model change. The recurrence model is deliberately the smallest viable (so it can't be accused of sprawl), and the differentiated investment is the live Now/Next/Clear behavior + progress bar + reopen-correctness that turns Bozz from an ICS viewer into a glanceable status board. Lightest model risk of the three, boldest UX payoff on the exact moment onboarding sells.

experiments.winner: Dedicated Class entity + timetable importer (the 'real timetable' power approach)

votes.preferred: Dedicated Class entity + timetable importer (the 'real timetable' power approach)
votes.why:
As Maya, a first-year, the moment that actually decides whether I adopt this is setup. I don't add one class at a time across the term, I get my whole timetable at once in week one (a printout or a copy-pasteable block from the uni portal) and I want it in fast. Variants A and C both make me hand-enter every class through the existing single-event form (CreateEventForm in src/components/sections/calendar/CalendarView.tsx, which currently has no repeat or room field at all), tick 'repeats weekly' each time, and pick start/end/colour 5-6 times over. That's exactly the tedium that makes me give up and just keep using my phone calendar. Variant B's paste-text importer ('BIO101 Tue 14:00-15:00 Room 3.21' one line per class, previewed before commit) is the only one that actually closes the 'build your timetable' gap the brief calls out, instead of leaving me a slightly nicer one-off event form. Thirty-second setup vs abandonment.

Variant B also matches my mental model: a class is 'BIO101, Tue 14:00, Room 3.21, every week,' not a calendar note that happens to repeat. The dedicated Mon-Fri grid lets me see my actual week the way it's printed on my timetable, and a clean code+room means the banner renders the richest, most useful 'Next class · BIO101 · 14:00 · Room 3.21 · in 18 min' (room is the thing I actually need before I leave). The teaching-week filter (weeks?: number[]) is a genuine UK-student need that A and C silently can't express: reading weeks and one-off cancelled lectures are real, and without it the recurring note keeps lying to me every reading week.

On engineering risk, B is heavier but not reckless: I checked calendar.ts and the projection discipline is identical to A and C. noteEvents() is already a pure per-note expander that Dashboard and CalendarView consume via eventsOnDay(), so classEvents(classes, horizon) slots in the same way, as additive local storage with no schema/sync/auth touch, staying inside the hard constraints. The new screen is the cost, but it's the part I'd actually use, and it's the natural home for the deferred Modules/grades layer later via ClassBlock.code.

I'd rank C second: the live Now/Next/Clear bar with reopen-correctness is the most delightful daily payoff and best serves 'your morning in 90 seconds,' and I'd want B to absorb that exact banner UX. But C still leaves me hand-entering my whole timetable, so the great banner has nothing to show until I've slogged through setup. A is the safest diff and a reasonable fallback, but as the actual user it solves the model author's sprawl worry, not my problem: it's the least ambitious option in an explicitly aggressive round, and overloading the generic note with a hidden repeatDay makes editing one week's cancelled class confusing. B wins because it makes the timetable genuinely first-class for the person living in it, not just representable.

votes.preferred: Live 'Now & Next' command bar (recurrence as a thin enabler, banner as the hero)
votes.why:
As Tom the freelancer, my "timetable" is recurring client calls, standing meetings, and a weekly review block, not a university course grid I can paste from a portal. That kills Variant B for me: its hero feature is the Mon-Fri grid plus the `BIO101 Tue 14:00 Room 3.21` paste-text importer, and I have nothing to paste. I'd be hand-typing into a heavier dedicated `classes` entity and a separate "My Timetable" screen I'd open once and never return to, paying for a parallel store and a new 'class' source for the smallest payoff to me. B is the right call for a student, the wrong call for a freelancer.

Variant A is the correct minimal model (I verified it in the code: noteEvents() already mirrors topicDeadlineEvents(), eventsOnDay() is the shared pipe at calendar.ts:48, nextEvent is already derived at TodayWidget.tsx:437, and CreateEventForm at CalendarView.tsx:57 has a clean slot for repeatDay/location). But A stops at parity: a banner fed off the existing nextEvent shows "Next: ..." which is basically what SummaryLine (TodayWidget.tsx:467) already narrates. Low marginal value.

C uses the *identical* minimal additive model as A (optional repeatDay+location on CalendarNote, location on CalendarEvent, expansion in noteEvents) so it carries A's exact backward-compat and no-sprawl guarantees that the code proves are real, but it spends the ambition where it actually changes my day: the live NOW state ("Now: client call, Room/Zoom, ends 15:00, 32 min left") with a progress bar, the amber-under-10-min countdown reusing the existing WARNING token, and the visibilitychange recompute so the bar is correct the instant I reopen the laptop. That reopen-correctness and the in-progress "am I in something right now" state are the genuinely glanceable status-board behaviors that deliver "your morning in 90 seconds" for someone whose schedule is meetings, not lectures. Same model risk as the safest option, boldest payoff on the exact moment the brief sells, and it stays inside every hard constraint (pure client-side projection, no store/schema/sync/auth/payments touched).

votes.preferred: Dedicated Class entity + timetable importer (the 'real timetable' power approach)
votes.why:
As Sam (power user), the thing that actually blocks me today isn't viewing one recurring class, it's getting my ENTIRE timetable into the app fast. Variant B's paste-text importer ("BIO101 Tue 14:00-15:00 Room 3.21" x 15 lines, regex-parsed in lib/calendar.ts, previewed before commit) is the only variant that solves the real job: seeding a full week in seconds instead of opening NoteForm 15 times and ticking "Repeats weekly" on each. That batch-entry + dedicated Mon-Fri grid editor is what makes the timetable feel first-class rather than bolted onto generic notes. A class is genuinely a distinct mental model (code + room + recurring + teaching-week filter), and ClassBlock gives it a clean typed home — including `weeks?: number[]` which A/C can't express at all, and which real university timetables need (reading weeks, alternating weeks). It's also the only defensible long-term anchor for the deferred Modules/grades layer (hangs off ClassBlock.code).

I verified the architecture supports all three: noteEvents() already flows through both Dashboard.tsx todayEvents/upcomingEvents (828-848) and CalendarView (734-738) via eventsOnDay(), and classEvents() would wire in identically with the 3 one-line additions the variant describes — same projection-not-storage discipline as A (no expanded events ever stored). The live <NextClassBanner> still ships, and renders its richest form here because ClassBlock has clean code+room fields.

ONE non-negotiable caveat that decides this for me: the new `classes` array MUST be added to SYNCED_KEYS in src/lib/sync.ts:17-34 (it's purely additive to the existing local-storage blob, NO Supabase schema/migration change — calendarNotes is already in that list and proves the pattern). The variant text says "no sync changes," but as a power user across laptop + desktop, a timetable that doesn't sync is a dealbreaker — I'd consider that a bug, not a feature. With classes added to that array it stays fully within the hard constraints (additive client storage only, no schema/migration/auth/payments touched) and syncs for free like notes do.

Why not A: minimal and provably backward-compatible, but it makes me hand-enter every class through a one-off-note form — it satisfies the data-model brief while missing the actual gap the brief calls out ("build your timetable, not just paste read-only ICS"). Why not C: the live Now/Next/Clear bar is genuinely the best part and I'd want B to adopt that exact 3-state hero + progress bar + visibilitychange reopen-correctness — but C deliberately keeps the model minimal, so it leaves the same fast-entry gap as A. B + C's banner would be my ideal; forced to pick one whole variant, B wins because entry speed and the timetable-as-a-concept are the power-user payoff, and the banner is additive on top.

changes.title: Wire the NL parser into the typed Quick-add widget submit (capture = triage = plan), with the zero-topic 'Create & file' chip
changes.approach:
QuickAddWidget.submit() currently does the dumb `addToInbox(v, deadline)` (line 19) with the raw string. Replace it with the exact pipeline QuickAddModal.submitTyped() already runs (QuickAddModal.tsx:74-109): on submit, call `parseVoiceTasks(text, topics)` (already imported across the app) to extract deadline + predicted topic per clause. Then branch on the result instead of dumping to Quicks:
  (a) High-confidence topic hit (task.topicId set): file straight into the topic via `ctx.addTopicItem(topicId, text, deadline)` (already on WidgetCtx, context.ts:17) and flash an inline confirm chip 'essay -> Bio -> Fri' built from task.text + topicName + deadlineLabel.
  (b) Dated but no topic match AND topics.length>0: route via `ctx.addDeadline(text, deadline)` (context.ts:23) exactly like DeadlineQuickAdd does.
  (c) ZERO-topic account (topics.length===0, so predictTopic returns null by design, taskParser.ts:263): infer a domain by exporting a small helper from taskParser.ts (`inferDomain(text): string|null`) that reuses the existing DOMAIN_KEYWORDS map (taskParser.ts:190-205) to map e.g. 'bio'/'essay'->study. Render a one-tap chip 'Create Bio topic and file this here'. On tap, call a NEW ctx callback `createTopicAndFile(domainLabel, text, deadline)` -> add it to WidgetCtx and implement in Dashboard.tsx by reusing makeBlankTopic + the addDeadline lazy-create path already at Dashboard.tsx:742-770 (generalise its 'Deadlines' bucket creation to take a name/keywords). The chip is dismissible -> falls back to addToInbox.
  (d) No date, no topic: keep `addToInbox` as today (no regression).
Use a small confirm-chip state (replace the single `flash` string with `{text, action?}`) so the user sees where it went and can undo to Quicks. Keep the DatePicker as an explicit override of the parsed date. This is pure reuse of shipped, tested parser logic; no new infra, no schema change. A dated task then auto-surfaces in TodayWidget.dueRows (TodayWidget.tsx:302-312) with zero planner ceremony.
changes.risk: Low. All logic already exists and is unit-exercised via QuickAddModal/DeadlineQuickAdd. Main risk is a too-eager auto-route on a weak topic score; mitigate by only auto-filing when task.topicId is set (predictTopic already enforces a score threshold of >1) and always surfacing the undo-to-Quicks chip. One new optional ctx callback must be threaded through both HomeView ctx spreads in Dashboard.tsx (lines 1534 and 1671); being optional keeps every other call site valid.

changes.title: Promote 'Today' to the zero-config default landing surface, demote the customizable grid to an optional 'Board'
changes.approach:
Today (TodayWidget) already computes the whole 90-second brief but is just one optional card a user must discover and place (DEFAULT_HOME is [] at registry.tsx:111). Reframe Home so the briefing is the hero and the react-grid becomes an opt-in 'Board'.
  - In HomeView, add a `surface: 'briefing' | 'board'` view-state (persisted to a new storage key e.g. 'homeSurface' via the existing getItem/setItem helpers used for homeBackground, HomeView.tsx:77-99 — NOT touching the saved homeLayout blob, so returning users' grids are byte-for-byte untouched, honouring the hard constraint).
  - Default a NEW account (homeItems.length===0 i.e. the current empty-state branch, HomeView.tsx:347) to surface='briefing': render a full-width TodayWidget directly (reuse renderCtx, pass widgetConfig {showEvents,showTasks} defaults) instead of HomeEmptyState. Keep the existing greeting() line above it.
  - Add a single segmented toggle in the existing top-right control row (next to Edit, HomeView.tsx:317-344): 'Briefing | Board'. 'Board' shows the current grid + empty-state/starter-templates exactly as today (power-user path, fully preserved).
  - Returning users with a non-empty saved homeLayout default to surface='board' so nothing changes for them; they gain the Briefing toggle as a bonus.
No widget-registry behaviour changes beyond confirming 'today' stays readyMulti (registry.tsx:79). Pure composition over existing WidgetCtx state.
changes.risk: Medium. The grid stays mounted via display toggling and measureBeforeMount (HomeView.tsx:351-356); rendering TodayWidget outside the grid is straightforward but must not unmount/remount the Grid when toggling surfaces (keep both mounted, display:none the inactive one, mirroring the existing Home-stays-mounted pattern at Dashboard.tsx:1509). Must verify TodayWidget renders sanely at full page width (it is column-flex, so fine). No auth/payments/schema touched.

changes.title: Rip out the vocabulary-gate onboarding; route cold-start AND skip onto the live briefing; demote tours to re-runnable 'learn more'
changes.approach:
Onboarding.tsx:322-339 still gates new accounts behind 'Four short walkthroughs ... Do them in order' that teach tool vocabulary ('A topic is an area of your life', SIDEBAR_STEPS line 125). Replace the 'Getting started' four-card gate with a compact outcome strip rendered on the briefing surface: three outcome steps that read off real state — (1) 'Add your timetable' (links into the existing WelcomeTimetable/AddFeedForm flow, calendarFeeds.length>0 marks it done), (2) 'Add your first 3 deadlines' (opens the priority-1 NL quick-add; counts dated topic items / addDeadline captures), (3) 'Here is your morning' (scrolls to / highlights the Today briefing). Drive completion off signals Dashboard already passes to Onboarding (Dashboard.tsx:1488-1503: topicCount, inboxCount, emailConnected, plus add a deadlineCount derived from topics).
  - The cold-start welcome flow already lands on a populated home (finishWelcome -> setActiveSection('home'), Dashboard.tsx:373-377). Ensure BOTH the cold-start path and skipColdStart/Skip-for-now (WelcomeTimetable.tsx:78) land on surface='briefing' from change #2 (the seeded topic + any pasted feed already fill it).
  - Keep the four spotlight tours (SIDEBAR_STEPS etc.) intact but only reachable via the existing replayWalkthroughs handler (Dashboard.tsx:392-399) surfaced as a 'Learn the app' link in Settings + the briefing's overflow — no longer a first-run gate.
changes.risk: Medium. The spotlight WalkEngine and replay plumbing stay; we only swap the first-run card and ensure the landing surface. Risk is the outcome-strip 'done' detection — reuse the already-wired StepCtx signals so there is no new polling. Must keep showOnboarding/onbDismissed persistence (Dashboard.tsx:386-416) working so the strip dismisses for good. No auth/payments/CI touched.

changes.title: Add a recurring-class model to CalendarNote, expanded client-side into CalendarEvents with a location field
changes.approach: CalendarNote (types.ts:282-293) is single-day-only with no recurrence/location, so a student can subscribe to a read-only ICS but cannot build 'BIO101, Tue 14:00, Room 3.21, every week'. Add BACKWARD-COMPATIBLE optional fields to CalendarNote: `recur?: { weekday: number; until?: number }` (weekday 0-6) and `location?: string`. Add an optional `location?: string` to CalendarEvent (types.ts:295-308). Then in lib/calendar.ts noteEvents(), when a note has `recur`, expand it CLIENT-SIDE across a forward window (e.g. today .. +120 days) the exact same synthesize-on-read way topicDeadlineEvents() already produces events (calendar.ts:5-25) — emit one CalendarEvent per occurrence with a deterministic id (`note:${n.id}:${occMs}`), carrying startMin/endMin/color/location. NON-recurring notes keep today's single-event behaviour untouched. Also pass through LOCATION from the ICS parser: ical.ts already has `ev.location` available from ical.js — add `location: ev.summary && ...` (set `location: ev.location || undefined`) at the two push sites (ical.ts:47-62) so subscribed-timetable events carry rooms too. Surface location in TodayWidget's timed-events rows (TodayWidget.tsx:202-223) as a muted ' · Room 3.21' suffix when present. No parallel store, no migration, no sync-blob bloat — directly answers the data-model-sprawl concern.
changes.risk: Medium. Recurrence expansion must be bounded (hard cap occurrences + a forward window) to avoid generating thousands of events; mirror the MAX_OCCURRENCES guard ical.ts already uses. eventsOnDay/upcomingEvents (Dashboard.tsx:828-848) consume noteEvents output unchanged, so expanded occurrences flow into Today + calendar automatically. All new fields optional => zero migration, existing notes/sync blobs stay valid. Touches no auth/payments/schema/sync.

changes.title: Build a lightweight class editor (recurring CalendarNote creator) — the 'real timetable' power approach
changes.approach: Give students who have no ICS link a way to TYPE a real timetable, building on change #4's recurring CalendarNote. Add an 'Add a class' affordance (a small form: title, weekday picker, start/end time, room/location, optional colour from colorBank) that creates a CalendarNote with `recur.weekday`, startMin/endMin, and `location`, pushed via the existing onCalendarNotesChange (already threaded through ctx, context.ts:42 and Dashboard's setCalendarNotes). Place the form inside CalendarView (the natural home for event creation; it already imports calendar helpers and owns calendarNotes editing) and ALSO expose it as a secondary option on WelcomeTimetable ('No link? Add your classes manually') so the first-run timetable step has a no-ICS path. Reuse AddFeedForm's field/colour styling helpers for visual consistency. Each saved class then expands via change #4 into Today + the calendar grid automatically.
changes.risk: Medium. Depends on change #4 landing first (shared recur/location fields). Must confirm CalendarView's existing note-editing path (it already renders noteEvents and supports add/edit) cleanly accepts the new optional fields without breaking the single-day editor — keep the recurring form additive, not a rewrite of the existing day-note editor. Pure local state via onCalendarNotesChange; no auth/payments/schema/sync.

changes.title: Add a persistent live 'Next class' banner to the Today briefing
changes.approach: TodayWidget already derives nextEvent (TodayWidget.tsx:436-437: the first timed event with start>=now, else the first of the day). Add a compact, prominent banner at the top of the briefing (above SummaryLine) that renders when nextEvent exists: 'Next: BIO101 · 14:00 · Room 3.21 · in 18 min'. Build the relative 'in N min' off Date.now() vs nextEvent.start with a 1-minute setInterval tick (a single useState heartbeat in the widget, cleared on unmount) so it stays live. Pull the room from the new CalendarEvent.location (change #4). Colour the banner with nextEvent.color and make it click-through to the calendar section via setActiveSection('calendar'). When nextEvent is in the past/ongoing, show 'Now: …' instead. This converts Bozz from a glorified ICS viewer into the one thing a student checks before leaving the house.
changes.risk: Low. nextEvent is already computed; this is presentational plus a self-contained interval timer. The only cross-dependency is location (change #4) — degrade gracefully to no-room when location is absent so the banner works even before #4/#5 ship. No data model or external surface touched.

plan.summary: Six grounded, bold-but-safe changes implementing this round's three agenda priorities with the user-chosen variants. (1) Wire parseVoiceTasks into QuickAddWidget.submit() — currently a dumb addToInbox at QuickAddWidget.tsx:19 — reusing the exact pipeline QuickAddModal.submitTyped already runs, plus the chosen zero-topic 'Create & file' chip driven by taskParser's DOMAIN_KEYWORDS. (2) Promote TodayWidget to a zero-config default 'Briefing' landing surface and demote the react-grid to an opt-in 'Board', via a new persisted surface flag that never touches the saved homeLayout blob. (3) Replace Onboarding's vocabulary-gate four-walkthrough card (Onboarding.tsx:322) with an outcome strip and route cold-start + skip onto the live briefing, demoting the spotlight tours to re-runnable 'learn more'. (4-6) Make the timetable first-class via the chosen dedicated-class approach: backward-compatible recurring + location fields on CalendarNote/CalendarEvent expanded client-side in lib/calendar.ts (mirroring topicDeadlineEvents), a lightweight type-a-class editor, and a live 'Next class' banner off TodayWidget's existing nextEvent. Every change is self-contained React/TS, reuses shipped logic, adds only optional fields (no migration), and touches no auth/payments/secrets/Supabase/CI. Changes 5-6 depend on 4 landing first. Recommended order: 1 -> 2 -> 3, then 4 -> 6 -> 5.
